channel.title: NICAR 2026 Conference Sessions
channel.description: Audio recordings from the NICAR 2026 data journalism conference, held March 5-8, 2026 in Indianapolis. Produced by Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE).
channel.itunes:author: IRE & NICAR
channel.itunes:summary: Audio recordings from the NICAR 2026 data journalism conference.

item.title: Quick-hit investigations
item.description:
Learn strategies, story ideas, resources and tips for watchdog stories on a variety of beats. You'll get tips on how to produce meaty enterprise stories that take days or weeks rather than months.
Speakers: Francisco Vara-Orta, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR
item.content:encoded:
Learn strategies, story ideas, resources and tips for watchdog stories on a variety of beats. You'll get tips on how to produce meaty enterprise stories that take days or weeks rather than months.

Type: Panel | Tracks: Story ideas | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Francisco Vara-Orta, Investigative Reporters & Editors
  • Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR
item.itunes:author: Francisco Vara-Orta, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Adam Rhodes, IRE & NICAR

item.title: Data-driven stories ideas for students
item.description:
Data-driven investigations don’t have to be long projects. In this panel, journalism educators will break down student data stories you can replicate in your own classroom or community. We’ll cover the data, the process and what can go wrong.
Speakers: Gerry Lanosga, Indiana University; Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Craig Lyons, Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260308-1335.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Data-driven investigations don’t have to be long projects. In this panel, journalism educators will break down student data stories you can replicate in your own classroom or community. We’ll cover the data, the process and what can go wrong.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Students, Story ideas | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Gerry Lanosga, Indiana University
  • Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors
  • Craig Lyons, Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism — Craig Lyons joined the Arnolt Center in November 2021 as its first managing editor. Lyons has spent nearly 20 years covering government agencies at the local, state and federal level in Michigan, Indiana, New Hampshire, Vermont and Maine. Lyons’ work has earned recognition from the Chicago Headline Club, Michigan Press Association, Hoosier State Press Association and the Indiana Chapter of the Society of Professional Journalists.

Resources

  • Slides
item.itunes:author: Gerry Lanosga, Indiana University; Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Craig Lyons, Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism

item.title: Use our quiz to focus on policy, not politics, in 2026
item.description:
Help your audience prioritize public policy over political party or personality in the 2026 elections with QuizTheVote. It will transform your existing candidate questionnaires into an interactive quiz in about an hour. We'll talk about how to prepare for and implement QuizTheVote, an open source resource built on Google sheets with no coding required.
Speakers: Evan Wyloge, Independent journalist; sandra fish, QuizTheVote
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260308-1058.pdf
Website: https://www.quizthevote.com/
item.content:encoded:
Help your audience prioritize public policy over political party or personality in the 2026 elections with QuizTheVote. It will transform your existing candidate questionnaires into an interactive quiz in about an hour. We'll talk about how to prepare for and implement QuizTheVote, an open source resource built on Google sheets with no coding required.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Elections | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Evan Wyloge, Independent journalist
  • sandra fish, QuizTheVote — Sandra Fish is a kinda retired data journalist specializing in politics. She's worked with the Colorado Sun, New Mexico In Depth and other news outlets, and she was a journalism instructor at the University of Colorado Boulder. In 2026, she's helping newsrooms implement QuizTheVote.

item.title: AI tools journalists can use ethically
item.description: Artificial intelligence is reshaping how media-related professionals work, make decisions, and tell stories. From content creation and audience analysis to workflow automation and risk management, AI is no longer theoretical— it is embedded in daily practice.This session focuses on how media professionals are using AI tools responsibly. Participants will explore real-world applications, understand limitations and risks, and discuss how to integrate AI without sacrificing clarity, credibility, or values.
item.content:encoded:
Artificial intelligence is reshaping how media-related professionals work, make decisions, and tell stories. From content creation and audience analysis to workflow automation and risk management, AI is no longer theoretical— it is embedded in daily practice.This session focuses on how media professionals are using AI tools responsibly. Participants will explore real-world applications, understand limitations and risks, and discuss how to integrate AI without sacrificing clarity, credibility, or values.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: AI, Tools & Tech | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Benét Wilson, IRE & NICAR
item.itunes:subtitle: Sunday, March 8, 1:00 PM ET

item.title: Uncovering wrongdoing with AI: Real-world methods and ethical boundaries
item.description:
In this panel, journalists who have deployed AI on cross-border investigations share practical techniques, limits and guardrails for using LLMs and machine learning in data reporting. Case studies include: scraping court records and classifying gender-based violence cases in Eswatini using GPT-assisted workflows; using a hybrid of OCR, regular expressions and LLMs to classify and review tens of thousands of environmental impact documents in France; and using machine-learning tools to detect deforestation and forest fraud across Southeast Asia by building on existing scientific research. Sharing reproducible workflows, panellists will show how to combine human judgment and feedback with machine assistance for document processing, pattern detection, classification and verification. The discussion will focus on human-in-the-loop systems, responsible disclosure of AI-assisted work and strategies for managing large document sets without compromising editorial credibility and scepticism. Attendees will leave with concrete methods for turning messy, unstructured data into searchable investigative resources, practical verification checklists and guidance on how to document and explain AI-assisted reporting to editors and audiences.
Speakers: Sotirios Sideris, Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University; Eva Constantaras, Lighthouse Reports; Sanjin Ibrahimovic, MuckRock; Jonathan Soma, Columbia University
item.content:encoded:
In this panel, journalists who have deployed AI on cross-border investigations share practical techniques, limits and guardrails for using LLMs and machine learning in data reporting. Case studies include: scraping court records and classifying gender-based violence cases in Eswatini using GPT-assisted workflows; using a hybrid of OCR, regular expressions and LLMs to classify and review tens of thousands of environmental impact documents in France; and using machine-learning tools to detect deforestation and forest fraud across Southeast Asia by building on existing scientific research. Sharing reproducible workflows, panellists will show how to combine human judgment and feedback with machine assistance for document processing, pattern detection, classification and verification. The discussion will focus on human-in-the-loop systems, responsible disclosure of AI-assisted work and strategies for managing large document sets without compromising editorial credibility and scepticism. Attendees will leave with concrete methods for turning messy, unstructured data into searchable investigative resources, practical verification checklists and guidance on how to document and explain AI-assisted reporting to editors and audiences.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: International | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Sotirios Sideris, Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University — Sotiris Sideris is a 2026 Nieman Fellow at Harvard and an investigative data journalist, editor and educator working on cross-border accountability investigations at the intersection of technology, power and the public interest. His work develops and scrutinizes data-driven and AI-assisted methods to expose systemic abuses and opaque institutions. He collaborates with international newsrooms and trains journalists in data reporting, OSINT and responsible AI.
  • Eva Constantaras, Lighthouse Reports
  • Sanjin Ibrahimovic, MuckRock — Developer Experience Engineer Sanjin Ibrahimovic is dedicated to building and supporting our developer community while enhancing the end-user and developer experience across MuckRock's offerings. His work includes writing documentation, drafting release notes, handling developer communications, developing new add-ons and improving MuckRock's APIs. He graduated cum laude in Computer Science from the Illinois Institute of Technology
  • Jonathan Soma, Columbia University
item.itunes:author: Sotirios Sideris, Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University; Eva Constantaras, Lighthouse Reports; Sanjin Ibrahimovic, MuckRock; Jonathan Soma, Columbia University

item.title: Turn the impossible into the doable: The random sample as a reporting tool
item.description:
You want to know how often people get their wages garnished because of unpaid medical bills...but it would take years to comb through all those court cases. Or maybe you have a long list of people deemed fraudsters and want to know how many of them are still operating businesses...but it would be impractical to look up each one. It's time for a random sample. This session addresses how to use random sampling as a reporting tool, and how to write up your findings for a lay audience. We'll review questions like: How big of a sample do you need? How can you design your random sample so that certain populations or geographies (like rural areas) don't get ignored?
Speakers: Rae Ellen Bichell, KFF Health News; Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News
item.content:encoded:
You want to know how often people get their wages garnished because of unpaid medical bills...but it would take years to comb through all those court cases. Or maybe you have a long list of people deemed fraudsters and want to know how many of them are still operating businesses...but it would be impractical to look up each one. It's time for a random sample. This session addresses how to use random sampling as a reporting tool, and how to write up your findings for a lay audience. We'll review questions like: How big of a sample do you need? How can you design your random sample so that certain populations or geographies (like rural areas) don't get ignored?

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Data analysis | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Rae Ellen Bichell, KFF Health News — Rae Ellen Bichell is a correspondent with KFF Health News and is based in Colorado. Her recent data work has focused on medical debt, federal grant cuts, Medicaid enrollment and doctor shortages. She used to be an on-air science journalist with NPR and a regional reporter with the Mountain West News Bureau. One time, she started a radio show in Helsinki.
  • Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News
item.itunes:author: Rae Ellen Bichell, KFF Health News; Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News

item.title: How to use AI responsibly
item.description:
Journalists across the world are trying to determine how and when to use GenAI and other machine learning techniques for reporting. Panelists from a range of projects and newsrooms will discuss how they considered journalistic ethics while using AI. Attendees should leave with ideas on when to combine AI classification with manual review, what to disclose to audiences and other practical tips.
Speakers: Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project; Sachita Nishal, Northwestern University; Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1034-1.pdf
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1034.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Journalists across the world are trying to determine how and when to use GenAI and other machine learning techniques for reporting. Panelists from a range of projects and newsrooms will discuss how they considered journalistic ethics while using AI. Attendees should leave with ideas on when to combine AI classification with manual review, what to disclose to audiences and other practical tips.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: AI | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project — Lisa Pickoff-White is the California Reporting Project’s director of research, based at Berkeley Journalism's Investigative Reporting Program. She turns police violence and misconduct records into data and impactful reporting with journalists and engineers. At KQED, she worked on award-winning investigations examining fire risk, emergency response breakdowns and jail conditions. She’s also built applications to meet audience needs, from fire maps to election results.
  • Sachita Nishal, Northwestern University — Sachita is a PhD candidate at Northwestern University, and her research asks: how can systems for finding, making sense of, and reporting on information support journalists' values and practices? New technologies like generative AI introduce value trade-offs: accuracy vs. efficiency, independence vs. algorithmic support, and so on. In response, she builds AI systems to surface these trade-offs and help journalists navigate them, in support of public-interest journalism.
  • Dylan Freedman, The New York Times
  • Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley — Katey Rusch is an investigative reporter at UC Berkeley’s Investigative Reporting Program, focused on police accountability and public records. A Pulitzer Prize finalist, she specializes in data-driven investigations.

Resources

  • Tipsheet
  • Slides
item.itunes:author: Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project; Sachita Nishal, Northwestern University; Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Fun with hypotheticals: Investigating your dubious data dilemmas in real time
item.description:
From the people who dared to ask “How many rats are in the Rainforest Cafe?” at NICAR25 comes an interactive panel on approaching a data story with an investigative mindset — this year done in real time. Attendees are encouraged to submit their most ridiculous investigative questions ahead of and during the panel. As a group, we’ll take a few of those questions and propose and refine a hypothesis, make a reporting plan, brainstorm records requests, and more. By the end of the session, participants will be equipped to craft an investigative data story from scratch — starting from even the most unexpected or whimsical question. Submit your ideas at https://tinyurl.com/hypotheticals26.
Speakers: Adiel Kaplan, Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures; Veronica Penney, American Association for the Advancement of Science; Emily Zentner, The California Newsroom
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1031.pdf
item.content:encoded:
From the people who dared to ask “How many rats are in the Rainforest Cafe?” at NICAR25 comes an interactive panel on approaching a data story with an investigative mindset — this year done in real time. Attendees are encouraged to submit their most ridiculous investigative questions ahead of and during the panel. As a group, we’ll take a few of those questions and propose and refine a hypothesis, make a reporting plan, brainstorm records requests, and more. By the end of the session, participants will be equipped to craft an investigative data story from scratch — starting from even the most unexpected or whimsical question. Submit your ideas at https://tinyurl.com/hypotheticals26.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas, tracks | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Adiel Kaplan, Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures — Adiel Kaplan is the program director for the Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures, where she focuses on how journalism can respond to and thrive as artificial intelligence disrupts news production, dissemination and consumption. She has worked as an investigative reporter and editor at local and national news outlets, including NBC News and the Miami Herald, and teaches investigative reporting for the investigative and data programs at Columbia Journalism School.
  • Veronica Penney, American Association for the Advancement of Science — Veronica Penney is a graphics editor and data reporter at Science magazine, where she reports, analyzes data and designs graphics for the newsroom. She specializes in data reporting, with an emphasis on climate change and cartography, and is an instructor for Columbia Journalism School’s Lede Program. Previously, she worked as an investigative data reporter for Colorado Public Radio and as a climate and graphics reporter for The New York Times and The Washington Post.
  • Emily Zentner, The California Newsroom — Emily Zentner is the data journalist for the statewide public and nonprofit media collaborative The California Newsroom, where she works with partner newsrooms on investigative stories and trains reporters to use data skills in their reporting. Her work primarily focuses on police accountability and climate. She was previously the data reporter at CapRadio in Sacramento, where she reported on wildfire, climate change and police mishandling of sexual assault cases.

Resources

  • Tipsheet
item.itunes:author: Adiel Kaplan, Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures; Veronica Penney, American Association for the Advancement of Science; Emily Zentner, The California Newsroom
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Elections 2026: Uncovering influence operations in the U.S.
item.description:
This panel will demonstrate how to use tools to investigate and track influence operations. Journalists will learn how to piece together opaque spending networks and uncover crucial details about operations through tax returns, Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA) filings, digital ad data, corporate records, lobbying disclosures and other sources.
Speakers: Anna Massoglia, MuckRock; Robert Maguire, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.content:encoded:
This panel will demonstrate how to use tools to investigate and track influence operations. Journalists will learn how to piece together opaque spending networks and uncover crucial details about operations through tax returns, Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA) filings, digital ad data, corporate records, lobbying disclosures and other sources.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Elections | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Anna Massoglia, MuckRock — Anna Massoglia is an investigative journalist and the editor of MuckRock. She leads the MuckRock News team and oversees its Sunlight Research Desk. Anna publishes Influence Brief and previously led OpenSecrets' media team, directing editorial processes and investigations. As a subject matter expert, she has worked with a wide range of newsrooms, academic institutions and nonprofits.
  • Robert Maguire, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington — Robert is the vice president for research and data at Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), where he leads efforts to investigate dark money and corruption. Prior to joining CREW, he founded the dark money tracking program at OpenSecrets, where he won SPJ’s Sigma Delta Chi award for his reporting. He has collaborated with and appeared in major outlets nationwide like NPR, The Washington Post, the New York Times and CNN.
item.itunes:author: Anna Massoglia, MuckRock; Robert Maguire, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Immigration data and how to make sense of it
item.description:
The Trump administration has drastically reshaped immigration enforcement. Newsrooms around the country are trying to make sense of what is happening to their communities. Join this panel to learn how reporters break down the most important immigration data sets and how that data can be used to tell both local and national stories.
Speakers: Will Craft, The Guardian; Leon Yin, Bloomberg News; Tanvi Misra, Independent journalist; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg
item.content:encoded:
The Trump administration has drastically reshaped immigration enforcement. Newsrooms around the country are trying to make sense of what is happening to their communities. Join this panel to learn how reporters break down the most important immigration data sets and how that data can be used to tell both local and national stories.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Will Craft, The Guardian — Will Craft is a data editor at the Guardian.
  • Leon Yin, Bloomberg News — Leon Yin is an investigative data journalist at Bloomberg News. He covers technology.
  • Tanvi Misra, Independent journalist
  • Elena Mejia, Bloomberg — Elena is a senior data visualization reporter and project lead for U.S. elections at Bloomberg News. She leads a team to execute the design and development of U.S. election results projects, collaborates with other reporters to analyze datasets related to election voting patterns and immigration, and creates visualizations for data-driven stories.
item.itunes:author: Will Craft, The Guardian; Leon Yin, Bloomberg News; Tanvi Misra, Independent journalist; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 4:30 PM ET

item.title: How to avoid a midterm meltdown: Tips for covering elections
item.description:
This panel will show how journalists navigate covering elections with data and how to avoid common mistakes. Speakers will share tips and stories about reporting on election results, campaign finance, and voter turnout.
Speakers: Phoebe Quinton, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Derek Willis, University of Maryland; Alexandra Glorioso, Miami Herald
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1215.pdf
item.content:encoded:
This panel will show how journalists navigate covering elections with data and how to avoid common mistakes. Speakers will share tips and stories about reporting on election results, campaign finance, and voter turnout.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Story ideas | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Phoebe Quinton, Atlanta Journal-Constitution — Phoebe Quinton is a data journalist at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, where she works on the politics and investigative teams. Previously, she worked on a special report at Reuters to investigate how a federal agency became an unlikely ally for companies and others responsible for cleanups at toxic sites.
  • Ence Morse, The Washington Post
  • Derek Willis, University of Maryland — Derek Willis teaches and does journalism, especially data journalism, at the University of Maryland. He also loves cricket.
  • Alexandra Glorioso, Miami Herald

Resources

  • Slides
item.itunes:author: Phoebe Quinton, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Derek Willis, University of Maryland; Alexandra Glorioso, Miami Herald

item.title: Data crowd competition: 40 ideas in 40 minutes
item.description:
This session invites attendees to compete for prizes for offering the coolest, most unique public datasets that every journalist should request. Each person gets one minute to come up to the mic and explain their dataset. The idea will be entered into a list of the screen, and then at the end attendees vote for their favorite. The top three win fabulous IRE merchandise.
Speakers: David Cuillier, Brechner FOI Project; Cierra Putman, WTHR; Samantha Sunne, Independent journalist
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1156.pdf
item.content:encoded:
This session invites attendees to compete for prizes for offering the coolest, most unique public datasets that every journalist should request. Each person gets one minute to come up to the mic and explain their dataset. The idea will be entered into a list of the screen, and then at the end attendees vote for their favorite. The top three win fabulous IRE merchandise.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas, Data analysis | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • David Cuillier, Brechner FOI Project — David Cuillier is director of the Brechner Freedom of Information Project at the University of Florida College of Journalism and Communications. He is a former journalist and taught journalism at the University of Arizona for 17 years. He is former president of the Society of Professional Journalists and the National Freedom of Information Coalition and co-author of "The Art of Access: Strategies for Acquiring Public Records."
  • Cierra Putman, WTHR
  • Samantha Sunne, Independent journalist — Samantha Sunne is the co-author of the textbook “Data + Journalism: A Story-Driven Approach to Learning Data Reporting,” now being published in its second edition. She is a freelance journalist based in New Orleans, specializing in data and investigative techniques. Sunne teaches at conferences, universities and newsrooms around the world.

Resources

  • Slides

item.title: Covering growing corporate power, even on deadline
item.description:
As corporate influence and power grows, it grows increasingly important to document the broader impact of business leaders' actions. For instance, companies are fined billions of dollars each year for stealing workers' wages, degrading the environment, defrauding the government, cheating consumers, and other misconduct - using Violation Tracker, reporters can look up the behavior of companies across more than 450 federal, state, and local regulatory agencies. Many of those same companies also receive billions in taxpayer subsidies in the name of economic development - using Subsidy Tracker, reporters can explore those tax breaks and other giveaways. Taken together, these two databases can help a reporter find patterns and trends in the ways businesses operate, and explore the ways governments enable poor behavior by corporate leaders to continue.
Speakers: Arlene Martinez, Good Jobs First
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1194.pdf
item.content:encoded:
As corporate influence and power grows, it grows increasingly important to document the broader impact of business leaders' actions. For instance, companies are fined billions of dollars each year for stealing workers' wages, degrading the environment, defrauding the government, cheating consumers, and other misconduct - using Violation Tracker, reporters can look up the behavior of companies across more than 450 federal, state, and local regulatory agencies. Many of those same companies also receive billions in taxpayer subsidies in the name of economic development - using Subsidy Tracker, reporters can explore those tax breaks and other giveaways. Taken together, these two databases can help a reporter find patterns and trends in the ways businesses operate, and explore the ways governments enable poor behavior by corporate leaders to continue.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Arlene Martinez, Good Jobs First — Arlene Martínez is with Good Jobs First, which promotes corporate and government accountability in economic development. GJF provides extensive research and technical assistance to policymakers, journalists and others studying the effectiveness of taxpayer subsidies for “economic development” as well as corporate behavior. Arlene previously reported at outlets including Hispanic Link News Service, the Los Angeles Times, The (Allentown) Morning Call and the USA TODAY Network.

Resources

  • Slides

item.title: Using open-source intelligence (OSINT) in investigations
item.description:
This session will show how journalists can begin incorporating open-source research methodologies in their investigations, with examples from real articles.
Speakers: Tristan Lee, Decoherence Media; Jennefer Harper, Decoherence Media; Alison Killing, Financial Times
item.content:encoded:
This session will show how journalists can begin incorporating open-source research methodologies in their investigations, with examples from real articles.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Research | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Tristan Lee, Decoherence Media
  • Jennefer Harper, Decoherence Media — Jennefer Harper is the co-founder of Decoherence Media, a nonprofit investigative media outlet. Her work focuses on far-right extremism in the US and transnational networks. She approaches her research and reporting through an intersectional framework using open and closed sources. She has a special interest in accelerationism, youth radicalization and government misconduct. In 2025, her work on a written investigation with The New Arab was shortlisted for an Amnesty Media Award.
  • Gabriel Geiger, Lighthouse Reports

item.title: Big Local News tools, wares and resources
item.description:
A lightning-round showcase of innovative newsroom tools and other resources that can help you collaborate on data journalism projects, harvest data and documents at scale, and track institutions for accountability reporting, from police agencies to state legislatures.
Speakers: Ryan Pitts, Big Local News; Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News; Gerald Rich, Big Local News; Serdar Tumgoren, Big Local News; Rosie Cima, Big Local News
item.content:encoded:
A lightning-round showcase of innovative newsroom tools and other resources that can help you collaborate on data journalism projects, harvest data and documents at scale, and track institutions for accountability reporting, from police agencies to state legislatures.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Tools & Tech | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Ryan Pitts, Big Local News
  • Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News — Cheryl Phillips is a lecturer at Stanford and founder of Big Local News, which supports newsrooms by sharing public data for accountability reporting. Last year, Big Local News contributed to a Pulitzer Prize for Local Reporting with The Baltimore Banner, The New York Times and nine other newsrooms. She spent 12 years at The Seattle Times, where she was a part of two Pulitzers for breaking news and two Pulitzer finalists. She is a former IRE board president.
  • Gerald Rich, Big Local News
  • Serdar Tumgoren, Big Local News
  • Rosie Cima, Big Local News
item.itunes:author: Ryan Pitts, Big Local News; Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News; Gerald Rich, Big Local News; Serdar Tumgoren, Big Local News; Rosie Cima, Big Local News

item.title: Becoming acquainted with your city as a data reporter with no beat
item.description:
Early-career, local data journalists might find themselves needing to quickly navigate the data ecosystem of a new place for the first time. In this session, panelists will share their tips and tricks for:- Learning what kinds of data are easily available in your new home- Building out a "beat" for yourself- Collaborating across multiple desks- working with a wide array of datasets
Speakers: Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner; Bianca Pallaro, The New York Times; Elliott Ramos, CBS News; Kara Newhouse, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
item.content:encoded:
Early-career, local data journalists might find themselves needing to quickly navigate the data ecosystem of a new place for the first time. In this session, panelists will share their tips and tricks for:- Learning what kinds of data are easily available in your new home- Building out a "beat" for yourself- Collaborating across multiple desks- working with a wide array of datasets

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner — Sahana Jayaraman is an award-winning data reporter at The Banner. She is responsible for both analyses that provide context to quick-turn stories and those that build the backbone of enterprise and investigative projects. Previously, she worked at The Arizona Republic and The Washington Post. She primarily works in R and Python.
  • Bianca Pallaro, The New York Times
  • Elliott Ramos, CBS News — Elliott Ramos a senior investigative data journalist for CBS News based in Chicago. He has worked at NBC News, WBEZ Chicago and The Wall Street Journal. He is a Lede Program alum and has taught at Medill school of journalism and Columbia College Chicago, which he graduated from in 2006.
  • Kara Newhouse, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism — ​​Kara is the deputy data editor at UMD's Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. She has 15 years of experience across newspapers, public radio and online outlets and has covered topics ranging from immigration to real estate to education. At her hometown paper in Pennsylvania, Kara’s investigation of a wealthy school district led to board member resignations, a more transparent superintendent search and the restoration of elementary art and music classes.
item.itunes:author: Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner; Bianca Pallaro, The New York Times; Elliott Ramos, CBS News; Kara Newhouse, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 3:15 PM ET

item.title: The price of recovery: How to investigate opioid settlement spending
item.description:
As states, counties, cities, and tribes begin spending more than $80 billion in opioid settlement funds, questions of transparency and accountability are increasingly urgent. In this session, five graduate students from West Virginia University’s Reed School of Media will share how they used investigative and data reporting techniques to track settlement spending in their state – and how this approach can be replicated in your communities! Panelists will outline key strategies for data collection, open records requests, and newsroom partnerships, while reflecting on journalism’s role in holding institutions accountable and amplifying community voices in responses to the opioid crisis.
Speakers: Ty McClung, West Virginia University; Jessica Riley, West Virginia University; Drew Solt, West Virginia University; Claudi Di Lima, West Virginia University; Hannah Heiskell, West Virginia University
item.content:encoded:
As states, counties, cities, and tribes begin spending more than $80 billion in opioid settlement funds, questions of transparency and accountability are increasingly urgent. In this session, five graduate students from West Virginia University’s Reed School of Media will share how they used investigative and data reporting techniques to track settlement spending in their state – and how this approach can be replicated in your communities! Panelists will outline key strategies for data collection, open records requests, and newsroom partnerships, while reflecting on journalism’s role in holding institutions accountable and amplifying community voices in responses to the opioid crisis.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Students, Story ideas | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Ty McClung, West Virginia University — Ty McClung is a second-year graduate student from Madison, West Virginia, who is pursuing a Master’s in Journalism at West Virginia University. He graduated from WVU in May of 2024 with his Bachelor of Science in Journalism with a focus in broadcast television production. Ty is currently serving as the television journalism program graduate assistant. Ty has experience reporting and anchoring in various capacities.
  • Jessica Riley, West Virginia University — Jessica Riley is a graduate student receiving both a bachelor's and a master's in journalism from West Virginia University, specializing in digital reporting. She has a strong passion for hard news/investigative work as well as data-driven stories.
  • Drew Solt, West Virginia University — Drew Solt is a second-year graduate student at West Virginia University. He is set to graduate with a Master's Degree in Journalism in May. Solt graduated in May of 2024 from WVU with a bachelor's degree in Journalism. He works as a graduate student at WVU Health Sciences Marketing and Communications and freelances in live sports production for WVU Athletics.
  • Claudi Di Lima, West Virginia University — Claudia is a second-year grad student in journalism, after graduating with a B.S.J. in May 2024. She is from Shepherdstown, West Virginia, and has worked many internships and part-time jobs as a multimedia journalist. She has received several awards for her reporting, including a ninth-place Hearst Award for television news and a third-place BEA of Festival Media Arts for a hard news story. Claudia loves her state and hopes to continue bringing it to life.
  • Hannah Heiskell, West Virginia University — Hannah is a second-year, 4+1 grad student pursuing a career in reporting. She graduates in May 2026 with a bachelors and masters degree in journalism. She’s from Morgantown, West Virginia, and has worked as a news reporter and opinion editor for The Daily Athenaeum. While she completes her masters, she also does freelance writing for local magazines, including GoXplore and the Tri-State Business Journal.
item.itunes:author: Ty McClung, West Virginia University; Jessica Riley, West Virginia University; Drew Solt, West Virginia University; Claudi Di Lima, West Virginia University; Hannah Heiskell, West Virginia University

item.title: Data team starter kit: How to build a data team from the ground up
item.description:
How do you set a new data team up for success? How do you grow a data team while maintaining your team's identity and standards? This session will tackle best practices for establishing and maintaining standards on new and growing data teams. We'll cover automating onboarding, standardizing project structure with Datakit and setting up a robust fact-checking process.
Speakers: Greg Morton, The Baltimore Banner; Allan James Vestal, The Baltimore Banner; Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner
item.content:encoded:
How do you set a new data team up for success? How do you grow a data team while maintaining your team's identity and standards? This session will tackle best practices for establishing and maintaining standards on new and growing data teams. We'll cover automating onboarding, standardizing project structure with Datakit and setting up a robust fact-checking process.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Career | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Greg Morton, The Baltimore Banner — Greg Morton is the data editor at the The Banner, where he leads a team of data journalists dedicated to pursuing ambitious lines of reporting, answering important questions and producing impactful journalism that informs and challenges readers.
  • Allan James Vestal, The Baltimore Banner
  • Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner — Sahana Jayaraman is an award-winning data reporter at The Banner. She is responsible for both analyses that provide context to quick-turn stories and those that build the backbone of enterprise and investigative projects. Previously, she worked at The Arizona Republic and The Washington Post. She primarily works in R and Python.
item.itunes:author: Greg Morton, The Baltimore Banner; Allan James Vestal, The Baltimore Banner; Sahana Jayaraman, The Baltimore Banner
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 2:00 PM ET

item.title: Data collection at scale for countries where data is hidden
item.description:
Learn about collecting data for countries where data is difficult to obtain, hidden, or behind wonky websites that don’t give you access to actual insights. Find out how to obtain data and support reporting pipelines - also how to gather data that’s more qualitative to supplement the numbers.
Speakers: Tazbia Fatima, Hearst; Alexander Borodikhin, Mediazona; Inaara Gangji, UC Berkeley; Eunjee Wi, Arizona State University
item.content:encoded:
Learn about collecting data for countries where data is difficult to obtain, hidden, or behind wonky websites that don’t give you access to actual insights. Find out how to obtain data and support reporting pipelines - also how to gather data that’s more qualitative to supplement the numbers.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: International, Data analysis | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Tazbia Fatima, Hearst
  • Alexander Borodikhin, Mediazona — Alexander Borodikhin is a reporter and editor at Mediazona, an independent Russian outlet covering political repression and the war in Ukraine. He heads the English-language site and reports for the data team. Mediazona, in collaboration with the BBC and a team of volunteers, has built a database of 200,000 verified Russian military casualties, data the Kremlin seeks to hide. The team has also scraped Russian courts’ websites to create a full searchable database.
  • Inaara Gangji, UC Berkeley — Inaara Gangji is a freelance journalist who has worked in Africa, the U.S. and the Middle East. She studied Journalism at Northwestern University and her work has appeared in the AP, Forbes, USA Today and Al Jazeera, centering on women, data, and social issues. She is currently finishing her Master's in Journalism at UC Berkeley and has been an Overseas Press Club of America Fellow, a Rosalynn Carter Journalism Fellow, and a White House Correspondents Association Scholar.
  • Eunjee Wi, Arizona State University — Eunjee Wi is a South Korean journalist and a Hubert H. Humphrey Fellow under the Fulbright Program at Arizona State University. She works at The Dong-A Ilbo, one of South Korea’s major national newspapers, where she served on its award-winning investigative unit, the Hero Content Team. She has worked as a project manager for interactive stories based on investigative reporting.
item.itunes:author: Tazbia Fatima, Hearst; Alexander Borodikhin, Mediazona; Inaara Gangji, UC Berkeley; Eunjee Wi, Arizona State University
item.itunes:subtitle: Saturday, March 7, 2:00 PM ET

item.title: Auditing AI algorithms for bias (and sometimes using AI tools to do so)
item.description:
AI is everywhere and reporters are finding new ways to hold AI tools accountable, sometimes using AI to do so. This panel will discuss how journalists can identify bias in predictive and generative AI tools built by some of the largest technology companies, plus speakers will share which AI tools they use for investigations and research.
Speakers: Sean Mussenden, University of Maryland; Daniel Trielli, University of Maryland; Tyler Dukes, McClatchy
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260307-1054.pdf
item.content:encoded:
AI is everywhere and reporters are finding new ways to hold AI tools accountable, sometimes using AI to do so. This panel will discuss how journalists can identify bias in predictive and generative AI tools built by some of the largest technology companies, plus speakers will share which AI tools they use for investigations and research.

Type: Panel | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: AI | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Hilke Schellmann, New York University
  • Sean Mussenden, University of Maryland — Sean Mussenden is interim director and data editor of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism, an investigative unit housed in the University of Maryland Philip Merrill College of Journalism.
  • Daniel Trielli, University of Maryland — Daniel Trielli is an assistant professor of media and democracy at the University of Maryland's Philip Merrill College of Journalism. He researches the impact of algorithmic systems on the distribution and production of journalism. He is interested in data and computational journalism, media literacy and algorithmic accountability. He occasionally writes about it in public-facing media, both in the United States and his native Brazil.
  • Tyler Dukes, McClatchy

Resources

item.title: The art of networking - and why it's vital
item.description: What networking isn't: Collecting business cards and letting them gather dust in your desk drawer. What netowrking is: A way to build two-way relationships that turn into long-term connections. This workshop offers tips on creating the perfect elevator pitch to introduce yourself, finding the right people to meet and starting to build mutually beneficial conversations that translate into opportunities, collaborators and purposeful long-term connections.

This workshop offers tips on creating the perfect elevator pitch to introduce yourself, finding the right people to meet and starting to build mutually beneficial conversations that translate into opportunities, collaborators and purposeful long-term connections.

item.title: How to do data-driven work in small communities
item.description:
The Data-Driven Reporting Project was founded to support newsrooms who might otherwise lack the tools, training or capacity to do more technically-focused investigative reporting. These are often smaller newsrooms with fewer resources. This in-person panel discussion at NICAR will show examples of how journalists, who have received support from the Data-Driven Reporting Project, are navigating complex and data-driven stories on small and underserved communities. Speakers will share stories and how they problem-solved connecting with the people behind the data. Good for beginners.
Speakers: Pam Dempsey, Northwestern University; Jill Blackman, Northwestern University; Aallyah Wright, Capital B News; Erica Peterson, Mountain State Spotlight; Chris Farone, Boston Institute for Nonprofit Journalism
item.content:encoded:

Speakers will share stories and how they problem-solved connecting with the people behind the data. Good for beginners.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas, Local | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Pam Dempsey, Northwestern University
  • Jill Blackman, Northwestern University
  • Aallyah Wright, Capital B News
  • Erica Peterson, Mountain State Spotlight
  • Chris Farone, Boston Institute for Nonprofit Journalism

item.title: Cheatsheet: An investigative spreadsheet tool powered by A.I.
item.description:
In this session, we will demo Cheatsheet, an internal tool developed at The New York Times that has helped in the reporting process for dozens of breaking news and investigative stories. Cheatsheet is a smart spreadsheet application that makes it quick and easy to analyze large datasets, document dumps and media collections at scale using A.I. systems. Cheatsheet gives reporters the ability to quickly find needles in large haystacks using recipes such as A.I. classification, translation, summarization, quote extraction and web search.
Speakers: Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times
item.content:encoded:

Cheatsheet is a smart spreadsheet application that makes it quick and easy to analyze large datasets, document dumps and media collections at scale using A.I. systems. Cheatsheet gives reporters the ability to quickly find needles in large haystacks using recipes such as A.I. classification, translation, summarization, quote extraction and web search.

Type: Demo | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: AI | Room: White River B

  • Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times

item.title: Behind the stories: 2025 Philip Meyer Winners
item.description:
A data deep dive into the 2025 Philip Meyer Award winners. Hear from reporters on how they gathered, cleaned, analyzed and visualized the data behind some of the year's biggest stories.
Speakers: Brant Houston, University of Illinois

A data deep dive into the 2025 Philip Meyer Award winners. Hear from reporters on how they gathered, cleaned, analyzed and visualized the data behind some of the year's biggest stories.

item.title: Teaching data journalism in the AI era
item.description:
As journalists, we spend years learning how to navigate data of all kinds and keep our investigations on track. Now students can ask an LLM to write their code or summarize a dataset in seconds... and get something back that looks right. Or let generative AI drive their assumptions about data without doing enough reporting. So how do we actually teach these foundational skills? While there's no finished playbook for this yet (or even consensus within individual schools or journalism departments), this panel will work through classroom strategies that let you use AI tools without short-circuiting critical thinking. We'll also get into which concerns about AI are real problems and which are overblown, plus where it might genuinely help students learn. You'll walk away with things to try in your next class, whether it's a new course or a curricular refresh.
Speakers: Alex Richards, Syracuse University; Matt Waite, University of Nebraska; Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media; Rahul Bhargava, Northeastern University
item.content:encoded:
As journalists, we spend years learning how to navigate data of all kinds and keep our investigations on track. Now students can ask an LLM to write their code or summarize a dataset in seconds... and get something back that looks right. Or let generative AI drive their assumptions about data without doing enough reporting. So how do we actually teach these foundational skills? While there's no finished playbook for this yet (or even consensus within individual schools or journalism departments), this panel will work through classroom strategies that let you use AI tools without short-circuiting critical thinking. We'll also get into which concerns about AI are real problems and which are overblown, plus where it might genuinely help students learn. You'll walk away with things to try in your next class, whether it's a new course or a curricular refresh.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: AI, Story ideas, Data analysis | Room: White River I

Speakers

  • Alex Richards, Syracuse University — Alex Richards is a journalism professor at Syracuse University’s Newhouse School. Before joining the academy, he was a reporter and editor at news organizations including the Chicago Tribune, The Chronicle of Higher Education and the Las Vegas Sun, as well as the consumer finance company NerdWallet. He's also a former IRE training director.
  • Matt Waite, University of Nebraska — Matt Waite is a journalism professor at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln who teaches courses in AI, data analysis and visualization. From 2007-11, he was a programmer/journalist for the St. Petersburg Times, where he developed PolitiFact, winner of the 2009 Pulitzer Prize for national reporting. Before that, he was an award-winning investigative reporter and co-author of "Paving Paradise: Florida's Vanishing Wetlands and the Failure of No Net Loss."
  • Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media — Elisabeth is a data journalist with American Public Media and a visiting journalist-in-residence at Indiana University.
  • Rahul Bhargava, Northeastern University — Rahul Bhargava (he/him/his) is a researcher, educator, designer, and artist working on creative data storytelling and computational journalism. He creates data murals and theatre with communities, award-winning museum exhibits, AI-powered civic technologies with CSOs, and delivers hands-on workshops and keynote talks across the globe. Rahul’s first book, “Community Data: Creative Approaches to Empowering People with Information”, is now available.
item.itunes:author: Alex Richards, Syracuse University; Matt Waite, University of Nebraska; Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media; Rahul Bhargava, Northeastern University
item.itunes:subtitle: Friday, March 6, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Investigating the Epstein Files
item.description: Speakers: Rob Barry, The Wall Street Journal; Jack Gillum, The Wall Street Journal; Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky, The Wall Street Journal; Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal; Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal
item.content:encoded:
Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Rob Barry, The Wall Street Journal
  • Jack Gillum, The Wall Street Journal — Jack Gillum is a reporter in The Wall Street Journal's Washington bureau, where he focuses on data-driven stories about politics and government. Jack has worked at Bloomberg News, ProPublica, the Washington Post, the Associated Press and USA Today. He has broken stories that included the existence and location of Hillary Clinton’s private email server, a U.S.-backed "Cuban Twitter" program and standardized test cheating in the nation's capital.
  • Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky, The Wall Street Journal — Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky is a data reporter for The Wall Street Journal. Before coming to The Journal, Jaclyn reported data-driven web and radio stories for WNYC/Gothamist, where her work on gun violence and COVID-19 won awards from the New York Press Club and the Deadline Club of New York. Jaclyn is an alumna of Cornell University and of the Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY, where she now teaches data journalism classes.
  • Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal — Caitlin Ostroff is a data reporter for The Wall Street Journal in New York, where she covers crypto, finance and politics. She was previously based in London, covering crypto and European finance for The Journal. She's landed scoops and chronicled turmoil at crypto exchanges FTX and Binance.
  • Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal — Brian Whitton is a computational journalist at The Wall Street Journal. He builds reporting tools and analyzes data for some of the Journal's most ambitious projects. If the numbers are big, he's probably involved somehow.
item.itunes:author: Rob Barry, The Wall Street Journal; Jack Gillum, The Wall Street Journal; Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky, The Wall Street Journal; Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal; Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal
item.itunes:subtitle: Friday, March 6, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Investigating TikTok through data donations
item.description: The Post collaborated with 1,100 readers to create a unique dataset of TikTok watch histories -- which they used to investigate political content, the app's addictiveness, and how the algorithm favors certain kinds of content over others. They also published the first Map of TikTok that hasn't been published yet but will hopefully break the internet. We'll discuss this project from beginning to end, including participant recruitment, secure/private data processing, and technical hurdles to digging into a giant dataset.
item.content:encoded:
The Post collaborated with 1,100 readers to create a unique dataset of TikTok watch histories -- which they used to investigate political content, the app's addictiveness, and how the algorithm favors certain kinds of content over others. They also published the first Map of TikTok that hasn't been published yet but will hopefully break the internet. We'll discuss this project from beginning to end, including participant recruitment, secure/private data processing, and technical hurdles to digging into a giant dataset.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Tools & Tech, Data analysis | Room: White River G
item.itunes:subtitle: Friday, March 6, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Easy visualizations for your first data stories
item.description:
Learn best practices for thinking about simple data visualizations to help tell your story – plus, a peek at some free tools!
Speakers: Adrian D. Garcia, Financial Times Specialist; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg; Haohsiang Ko, Financial Times; Prinz Magtulis, Reuters
item.content:encoded:
Learn best practices for thinking about simple data visualizations to help tell your story – plus, a peek at some free tools!

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Data viz | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Adrian D. Garcia, Financial Times Specialist — Adrian D. Garcia has been the managing editor of data visualizations for Financial Times Specialist since January 2024. His team covers topics related to corporate boards, asset and wealth management and insurance. In the fall, he teaches an intro to data journalism course at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY. He also serves on the NAHJ Business Journalism Task Force.
  • Elena Mejia, Bloomberg — Elena is a senior data visualization reporter and project lead for U.S. elections at Bloomberg News. She leads a team to execute the design and development of U.S. election results projects, collaborates with other reporters to analyze datasets related to election voting patterns and immigration, and creates visualizations for data-driven stories.
  • Haohsiang Ko, Financial Times — Haohsiang is a data journalist on the FT’s visual and data journalism team based in Hong Kong, covering Asia news stories through data analysis and graphics. Before joining the FT, he led the data journalism team at The Reporter in Taiwan, collaborating with journalists on investigative and in-depth stories.
  • Prinz Magtulis, Reuters — Prinz has been a graphics reporter for Reuters since 2022, designing charts and other visuals to better tell stories behind financial and economic data. Prior to this, he was based in the Philippines, where he worked as an economic beat journalist for over a decade covering macroeconomy, public finance and the financial markets for various local and international outlets. His favorite chart -- and breakfast-- is a waffle.
item.itunes:author: Adrian D. Garcia, Financial Times Specialist; Elena Mejia, Bloomberg; Haohsiang Ko, Financial Times; Prinz Magtulis, Reuters
item.itunes:subtitle: Friday, March 6, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Nonprofit data 101
item.description:
There's a good nonprofit story waiting to be written on every reporting beat and in every coverage area, if you know where to look. This session will teach you how to navigate the world of nonprofit data and documents. To gain tax-exempt status, organizations like hospitals, charter schools, colleges and humane societies must agree to report annual information on their finances, compensation of key personnel, conflicts of interest and more. If you know how to cut through the accounting language to find what you're looking for, these annual filings — and other documents generated by a nonprofit — can answer questions big and small and add useful context to your reporting.
Speakers: Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica; Ellis Simani, ProPublica
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1133.pdf
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1133-1.pdf
item.content:encoded:
There's a good nonprofit story waiting to be written on every reporting beat and in every coverage area, if you know where to look. This session will teach you how to navigate the world of nonprofit data and documents. To gain tax-exempt status, organizations like hospitals, charter schools, colleges and humane societies must agree to report annual information on their finances, compensation of key personnel, conflicts of interest and more. If you know how to cut through the accounting language to find what you're looking for, these annual filings — and other documents generated by a nonprofit — can answer questions big and small and add useful context to your reporting.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Story ideas | Room: White River I

Speakers

  • Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica — Andrea Suozzo is a news apps developer at ProPublica, where she runs Nonprofit Explorer and does data reporting on nonprofits, maternal health and other topics.
  • Ellis Simani, ProPublica — Ellis is a data reporter at ProPublica, where his work pairs quantitative analysis with traditional investigative reporting techniques. He often covers stories at the intersection of money and power. His recent work has interrogated the ways that nonprofit organizations raise and spend their money. Simani has also investigated the stock trading of the wealthiest Americans. Before joining ProPublica in 2019, he worked on the Los Angeles Times’ data visualization desk.

item.title: How we did it: Student-led investigations
item.description:
Are you a student journalist who wishes they had a clearer roadmap for getting started with accountability journalism? Let's talk about open records laws, data analysis, tools and what's available to you, even if you're at a private institution. This session is great for total beginners or those looking for story ideas.
Speakers: Haley Parsley, Capital News Service; Andrew Miller, Indiana Daily Student; Sana Muneer, Boston University; Matthew Wuschke, Boston University; Elizabeth Alspach, University of Maryland
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1233-1.pdf

item.content:encoded:
Are you a student journalist who wishes they had a clearer roadmap for getting started with accountability journalism? Let's talk about open records laws, data analysis, tools and what's available to you, even if you're at a private institution. This session is great for total beginners or those looking for story ideas.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Students | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Haley Parsley, Capital News Service — Haley is a fellow at the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism and a graduate student at the Philip Merrill College of Journalism. She currently reports for Capital News Service, a newswire run by the University of Maryland, and is based in Washington, D.C. She holds a bachelor’s degree from Pomona College.
  • Andrew Miller, Indiana Daily Student — Andrew Miller has served as co-editor-in-chief for the Indiana Daily Student since August 2025. There, he’s previously held the positions of science and environmental reporter, elections reporter, news editor and managing editor. In summer 2024, he interned at The Oregonian in Portland, Oregon, reporting on business and the timber industry. In summer last year, he reported general assignment at The Herald-Times.
  • Sana Muneer, Boston University — Sana Muneer is a junior at Boston University studying journalism and psychology. Most recently, she served as the general assignment nightside news co-op at Boston.com. She is currently vice president of BU’s independent student newspaper, The Daily Free Press, and also held roles as managing co-editor and features editor. Her work can be found in The Dorchester Reporter, The Marblehead Current, BU Today, Student Press Law Center and The Stroll Glen Ellyn.
  • Matthew Wuschke, Boston University — Matthew Wuschke is a junior at Boston University studying journalism with an focus on sports and data journalism. He covers athletics for Boston University's student organization WTBU Sports, and has experience in writing, radio, photography and graphic design. He has skills in data extraction and analysis with a interest in Artificial Intelligence and how it can be used effectively in newsrooms.
  • Tabitha Hidalgo, University of North Texas — Tabitha Hidalgo is an investigative-minded journalist and incoming May 2026 graduate of the University of North Texas. She has led student newsrooms as editor-in-chief of the North Texas Daily and San Antonio College’s Sundial, and currently serves as president of UNT’s NAHJ chapter. She is passionate about storytelling that elevates underrepresented voices.
  • Elizabeth Alspach, University of Maryland — Lizzy Alspach is a senior journalism and Spanish major at the University of Maryland. There, she guided data analysis in the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism’s "Off the Rails" project, which revealed how hazardous materials on trains face minimal regulations. She’s also editor in chief of The Diamondback, a university independent student newspaper.

Resources

  • Tipsheet
  • Slides

item.title: Everything you never wanted to know about crypto: A primer for data investigations
item.description:
This panel will cover the basics you need to understand about crypto to start covering it, including how it works, what is the blockchain, where to find data and where to find experts. We'll put a heavy emphasis on prediction markets, the Trump family's crypto businesses and finding local stories.
Speakers: Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal; Jeremy Merrill, The Washington Post; Sam Kessler, The Wall Street Journal
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1122.pdf
CoLab notebook: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1CbNQY1IhY5ShxrNEhELM6Iymi2uWzEyI?authuser=1#scrollTo=V5t8GJ1ZlOkK
item.content:encoded:
This panel will cover the basics you need to understand about crypto to start covering it, including how it works, what is the blockchain, where to find data and where to find experts. We'll put a heavy emphasis on prediction markets, the Trump family's crypto businesses and finding local stories.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas, Research | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Caitlin Ostroff, The Wall Street Journal — Caitlin Ostroff is a data reporter for The Wall Street Journal in New York, where she covers crypto, finance and politics. She was previously based in London, covering crypto and European finance for The Journal. She's landed scoops and chronicled turmoil at crypto exchanges FTX and Binance.
  • Jeremy Merrill, The Washington Post — Jeremy Merrill is a data reporter writing primarily about technology at The Washington Post. He lives in Atlanta.
  • Sam Kessler, The Wall Street Journal — Sam Kessler is a reporter for The Wall Street Journal based in New York City. He focuses on the intersection of power, business and technology, using data analysis and programming alongside traditional reporting to uncover investigative stories. Previously, Sam covered the crypto industry as deputy managing editor at CoinDesk, where he was part of a team that won a 2023 Gerald Loeb Award for its coverage of Sam Bankman-Fried and FTX.

Resources

item.title: Tracking hidden money, influence and power across borders
item.description:
From offshore accounts to complex layers of companies, this session will show you how to follow it across borders and back again, using public data available to any journalist. This session will show practical ways to trace hidden owners, foreign ties, and influence networks using public records and open data. Attendees will learn proven methods to follow the money, connect global disclosures, and turn complex data trails into clear, high-impact stories.
Speakers: Anna Massoglia, MuckRock; Ezana Ćeman, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project; Klil Eden, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1219.pdf
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1219-1.pdf
item.content:encoded:
From offshore accounts to complex layers of companies, this session will show you how to follow it across borders and back again, using public data available to any journalist. This session will show practical ways to trace hidden owners, foreign ties, and influence networks using public records and open data. Attendees will learn proven methods to follow the money, connect global disclosures, and turn complex data trails into clear, high-impact stories.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, International, Story ideas | Room: White River I

Speakers

  • Anna Massoglia, MuckRock — Anna Massoglia is an investigative journalist and the editor of MuckRock. She leads the MuckRock News team and oversees its Sunlight Research Desk. Anna publishes Influence Brief and previously led OpenSecrets' media team, directing editorial processes and investigations. As a subject matter expert, she has worked with a wide range of newsrooms, academic institutions and nonprofits.
  • Ezana Ćeman, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project — Ezana Ćeman is the Product Manager for data platforms at OCCRP, where she oversees the development of tools like OCCRP Aleph Pro. Since 2021, she has focused on user-centered innovation to support the organization’s strategic goals. Ezana holds a master’s in IT and is a Grace Hopper and two-time Forbes 30 Under 30 Scholar. Additionally, she leads digital education and advocacy at FemLab, an NGO advancing gender equality in Bosnia and Herzegovina.
  • Klil Eden, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project — Klil is the head of engineering for data platforms at the Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project. He leads a team of developers and data engineers creating data-intensive, secure, cloud-native platforms for investigative research. Klil's background spans software development, cybersecurity and nonprofit technology strategy. He has worked on mission-driven sectors ranging from risk management, AML, physical security, journalism and disinformation.

Resources

item.title: Persuasion 401: The psychology of investigative reporting
item.description:
This training provides a deep dive into the psychology of persuasion so that reporters can increase their odds in getting record custodians to cough up data, reluctant sources to talk, and editors to support their projects. Tips will be research-based and help attendees learn the power of the mind. Note: Use these strategies for good, not evil!
Speakers: David Cuillier, Brechner FOI Project
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1157.pdf

Tips will be research-based and help attendees learn the power of the mind. Note: Use these strategies for good, not evil!

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Public records | Room: White River G

Speakers

He is a former journalist and taught journalism at the University of Arizona for 17 years. He is former president of the Society of Professional Journalists and the National Freedom of Information Coalition and co-author of "The Art of Access: Strategies for Acquiring Public Records."

item.title: How to find data
item.description:
A conversation about how to find and vet credible data sources that you can use right away.

Speakers

  • Greg Morton, The Baltimore Banner — Greg Morton is the data editor at the The Banner, where he leads a team of data journalists dedicated to pursuing ambitious lines of reporting, answering important questions and producing impactful journalism that informs and challenges readers.
  • Stephanie Lamm, Atlanta Journal-Constitution — Stephanie Lamm is a data reporter at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution who uses public records, data analysis and shoe-leather reporting to hold institutions accountable and uncover information that would otherwise remain hidden. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and spent five years working in Texas before landing in Atlanta.
  • Sean Mussenden, University of Maryland — Sean Mussenden is interim director and data editor of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism, an investigative unit housed in the University of Maryland Philip Merrill College of Journalism.

item.title: Analyzing images and videos with AI
item.description:
Tools like facial recognition, red-light/speed cameras and crowd-counting have long been the territory of locked-up, super-secret algorithms from private industry and government. But no longer! Learn how to use open-source tools like the Python library supervision and open-source models to unlock real-world analysis abilities. Automate analysis of almost everything – from satellite imagery to TikTok videos – and unlock levels of small-team investigation that were impossible just a few years ago. Learn how AI can sort through images and video to help you wrangle footage from protests and riots (NYT), analyze trends on TikTok (Washington Post), keep an eye on your local school board meetings (Hearst), measure the effects of congestion pricing (Bloomberg), and a hundred other tidbits for when the cameras might be rolling. With a little Python and a dash of foundational knowledge, this session will tackle downloading videos, building and evaluating transcripts, splitting scenes, categorizing images, and detecting/counting/tracking objects.
Speakers: Jonathan Soma, Columbia University
Walkthrough: https://jsoma.github.io/workshop-ai-images-video/nicar-2026/

With a little Python and a dash of foundational knowledge, this session will tackle downloading videos, building and evaluating transcripts, splitting scenes, categorizing images, and detecting/counting/tracking objects.

item.title: Accessibility basics
item.description:
Come to this session for a crash course in some of the issues to consider when meeting basic accessibility requirements for digital news. Panelists will discuss topics including color accessibility, alt text, mouseless navigation, and more.
Speakers: Stacy Kess, Equal Access Public Media; Emilia Ruzicka, Independent journalist; Patrick Garvin, Maritz Global Events
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1096.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Come to this session for a crash course in some of the issues to consider when meeting basic accessibility requirements for digital news. Panelists will discuss topics including color accessibility, alt text, mouseless navigation, and more.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Equity, inclusion and accessibility | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Stacy Kess, Equal Access Public Media — Stacy Kess is the founder and chief of editorial of Equal Access Public Media (EAPM). She is a reporter and editor with more than 25 years’ experience and has contended with life-long health issues. She is also a survivor of a head-on car-crash in 2017. She credits this as the beginning of EAPM. Kess said this led her to her mission of decreasing the accessibility barriers in the news. Kess oversees editorial and media at EAPM. She currently lives in Boston, Massachusetts.
  • Emilia Ruzicka, Independent journalist — Emilia Ruzicka is a data journalist seeking their next full-time job. They work part-time managing data for the Trans News Initiative and were formerly a data reporter at Stacker. They have published with the Urban Institute, the Center for News, Technology, and Innovation, and other outlets. Emilia enjoys reporting on health, science, technology, queer issues and public data. They have an MA from U. of Virginia and a BS from Brown U.
  • Patrick Garvin, Maritz Global Events

Resources

item.title: Utilizing new police misconduct and employment databases
item.description:
Learn about using new, groundbreaking databases to track police misconduct and employment histories: the Police Records Access Project in California, ACLU of West Virginia/Dragline's Police Accountability Dashboard, the New York Police Disciplinary Records database, and the National Police Index. This hands-on session will also briefly cover other related police data projects around the country. This session is good for those with some experience using spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.
Speakers: Sam Stecklow, Invisible Institute; Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project; Maheen Khan, Invisible Institute; Kyle Vass, Dragline (ACLU-WV); Beryl Lipton, Democrat & Chronicle
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260306-1021.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Learn about using new, groundbreaking databases to track police misconduct and employment histories: the Police Records Access Project in California, ACLU of West Virginia/Dragline's Police Accountability Dashboard, the New York Police Disciplinary Records database, and the National Police Index. This hands-on session will also briefly cover other related police data projects around the country. This session is good for those with some experience using spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Local, Public records, Research, Tools & Tech, Story ideas | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Sam Stecklow, Invisible Institute — Sam Stecklow is an investigative journalist and FOIA fellow with Invisible Institute, a nonprofit public accountability journalism organization based in Chicago. He works on investigations into police misconduct and abuse, and on II's public data tools the Civic Police Data Project and the National Police Index. His work has been recognized with a Sunshine Award from Utah SPJ and a Driehaus Award for investigative journalism. He is currently based in Orlando.
  • Lisa Pickoff-White, California Reporting Project — Lisa Pickoff-White is the California Reporting Project’s director of research, based at Berkeley Journalism's Investigative Reporting Program. She turns police violence and misconduct records into data and impactful reporting with journalists and engineers. At KQED, she worked on award-winning investigations examining fire risk, emergency response breakdowns and jail conditions. She’s also built applications to meet audience needs, from fire maps to election results.
  • Maheen Khan, Invisible Institute — Maheen Khan is the director of technology at the Invisible Institute. She studied information analysis and computer science in undergrad, and now works to maintain the Invisible Institute's tech tools (like CPDP) and support with data analysis for investigations.
  • Kyle Vass, Dragline (ACLU-WV) — Kyle Vass is an investigative journalist who runs a not for profit news site (Dragline) in West Virginia. His work focuses on government accountability with an emphasis on police misconduct.
  • Beryl Lipton, Democrat & Chronicle

Resources

  • Slides

item.title: Tips to investigate influencers and live-streamers
item.description:
This session centers around the tools used for three recent stories involving video analysis.The reporting subjects include a deep dive into livestreamers promoting crypto casinos, political podcasters in the run-up to the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election and linking the advertisers propping up right-wing influencers.Through these case studies, we will highlight tools to archive videos, inspect metadata, analyze transcripts, map guest networks, and detect text and objects in video.Come learn some new tools for your next investigation!
Speakers: Leon Yin, Bloomberg News
