item.title: Five demos for finding things with A.I. outside of chatbots
item.description:
The most exciting new A.I. tools don't involve chatbots. We all have big documents and datasets. Sometimes we think we know approximately what's inside — but we just need to find it. In this session, we'll show you five more technical ways that A.I. can be used to supercharge the investigative process. We will demo semantic search for text, spreadsheets and images; embedding and clustering; and identifying visual objects in media — all in tools you can use yourself to solve real journalistic problems, without having to endure ChatGPT's excessive use of em-dashes and its other limitations.
Speakers: Dylan Freedman, The New York Times; Duy Nguyen, The New York Times; Jeremy Merrill, The Washington Post; Angela Eichhorst, CT Mirror
GitHub repo: https://github.com/freedmand/semantra
GitHub repo (2): https://github.com/ang-eichhorst/legitalk
item.content:encoded:
The most exciting new A.I. tools don't involve chatbots. We all have big documents and datasets. Sometimes we think we know approximately what's inside — but we just need to find it. In this session, we'll show you five more technical ways that A.I. can be used to supercharge the investigative process. We will demo semantic search for text, spreadsheets and images; embedding and clustering; and identifying visual objects in media — all in tools you can use yourself to solve real journalistic problems, without having to endure ChatGPT's excessive use of em-dashes and its other limitations.

Type: Demo | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: AI | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Dylan Freedman, The New York Times
  • Duy Nguyen, The New York Times — Duy Nguyen is a data scientist and engineer for The New York Times A.I. Initiatives team, where he leads original research and tooling to unlock new reporting and production capabilities, while maintaining responsible A.I. use. Before that, he helped local media solve business and audience problems with A.I. at Columbia Journalism School. He was previously a data journalist at The Times and NPR and briefly contributed to DocumentCloud.
  • Jeremy Merrill, The Washington Post — Jeremy Merrill is a data reporter writing primarily about technology at The Washington Post. He lives in Atlanta.
  • Angela Eichhorst, CT Mirror — Angela Eichhorst is CT Mirror's first AI data reporter / product developer, where she is focused on developing internal newsroom tools for investigative reporting and data analysis for stories. Previously she was a student journalist, fact-checked at CNN, and produced polls at the AP-NORC Center, including VoteCast. In her free time, Angela enjoys exploring Connecticut and playing boardgames.

Resources

item.title: Editing the machine: Fact-checking AI-assisted reporting in the newsroom
item.description:
As AI tools move into daily reporting, the real challenge isn't keeping up with the hype, it's the editing. This session is focused on the real editorial challenges that come up when you're responsible for people, data, ethics, and deadlines all at once—and AI is ambient and often invisible. This isn’t a heady conversation about the future of AI in journalism, ethics boards or tech tips. It’s meant to focus on what's already changed and how that shows up in newsroom decision-making. The goal is to move beyond the abstract and talk about everyday editorial decisions. What feels different in copy review? In sourcing? In data analysis? Where do editors need to slow down, ask a different question, or reopen a framing that might have quietly solidified? This is about how editorial judgment evolves when AI is in the workflow.
Speakers: Angeliki Kastanis, Associated Press; Jennifer LaFleur, UC Berkeley School of Journalism; Meghan Hoyer, The Washington Post

item.title: What your newsroom knows (but can’t find)
item.description:
Newsroom archives hold decades of reporting. They represent thousands of sources, events and institutional knowledge built over the years. And yet they’re incredibly unwieldy and difficult to leverage. How many of you search your own newsroom’s archives through Google? In this panel, we'll walk you through how we built Subline, an end-to-end system that extracts knowledge graphs from newsroom archives. We’ll demonstrate how it supports smarter archive search, article link prediction, multi-step investigative queries, and better reader-facing recommendations.
Speakers: Avery Wallace Louis, Stanford University; Hannah Woodworth, Stanford Univeristy; Lucy Zimmerman, Stanford University
item.content:encoded:
Newsroom archives hold decades of reporting. They represent thousands of sources, events and institutional knowledge built over the years. And yet they’re incredibly unwieldy and difficult to leverage. How many of you search your own newsroom’s archives through Google? In this panel, we'll walk you through how we built Subline, an end-to-end system that extracts knowledge graphs from newsroom archives. We’ll demonstrate how it supports smarter archive search, article link prediction, multi-step investigative queries, and better reader-facing recommendations.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Tools & Tech, Story ideas | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Avery Wallace Louis, Stanford University — Avery is a masters student in Symbolic System at Stanford. Her research is primarily on computational models of collective learning, but her other (FAR MORE FASCINATING) gig is working on Subline-- a system for extracting knowledge graphs from newsroom archives and data dumps (with Hannah and Lucy). Come talk to her about graphs, datadumps and what your dream tool for sifting through them is, evolution, complex systems, and Quine (or whatever else floats your boat).
  • Hannah Woodworth, Stanford Univeristy — Hannah is a data journalist and researcher based in Stanford, California. She most recently contracted as an AI specialist at Reuters, where she previously worked on the data team. At Stanford, she is developing knowledge graphs to structure complex news archives.
  • Lucy Zimmerman, Stanford University — Lucy Zimmerman is a master's student in computer science at Stanford University focused on technology for social impact. She has conducted research on the societal impacts of generative AI at Stanford HAI and contributed to projects spanning computational journalism, civic tech and human-centered machine learning. Lucy is passionate about building tools that strengthen democratic institutions and expand access to critical information.
item.itunes:author: Avery Wallace Louis, Stanford University; Hannah Woodworth, Stanford Univeristy; Lucy Zimmerman, Stanford University
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 10:00 PM ET

item.title: We're managers, ask us anything: career, networking, mentorship
item.description:
Name just about any kind of newroom, job, story or industry crisis and these powerhouse journalists have been there, done it and survived - stronger and better than ever! They'll share all their best tips for working through tough times and making it as a journalist, finding and telling the best stories, how to make your IRE membership work for you and more. We'll save lots of time at the end for questions and some lively networking before the opening reception.
Speakers: Cindy Galli, MS NOW; Kate Howard, Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting; Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors

Previously, she was executive producer of the investigative unit at ABC News, where her team garnered national awards including a duPont-Columbia baton and a Peabody nomination. Cindy has served on the IRE board of directors since 2019. Cindy was born and raised in the San Francisco Bay Area and is a graduate of the University of California at Berkeley.

item.title: Monitoring police accountability laws across the U.S.
item.description:
Led by California and New York, states across the U.S. have begun adopting laws to give the public access to police accountability data, including use-of-force and discipline records. But what’s available varies by state, and there are no central repositories for tracking problem police officers (and some agencies fail to access what is available). In addition, laws that are intended to make sure problem police never work in law enforcement again are riddled with inconsistent application. This panel will take a deeper look at the documents and data across the country, and the stories reporters have done in multiple states to illuminate what has previously been shielded from the public. We dig into how we went about forming news/university/private partnerships to report on unevenly enforced police discipline, and how you could execute a similar project with the data and documents in your state. This panel was planned in conjunction with "Utilizing new police misconduct and employment databases," scheduled for Friday at 9:00 a.m. This panel will focus on changing laws, data partnerships and developing stories; the next panel will give specific how-tos using available data.
Speakers: Jodi Upton, Syracuse University; Sam Stecklow, Invisible Institute; Sarah Taddeo, USA TODAY Network; David Robinson, Rochester Democrat and Chronicle; Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1304-1.pdf
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1304.pdf

Led by California and New York, states across the U.S. have begun adopting laws to give the public access to police accountability data, including use-of-force and discipline records. But what’s available varies by state, and there are no central repositories for tracking problem police officers (and some agencies fail to access what is available). In addition, laws that are intended to make sure problem police never work in law enforcement again are riddled with inconsistent application. This panel will take a deeper look at the documents and data across the country, and the stories reporters have done in multiple states to illuminate what has previously been shielded from the public. We dig into how we went about forming news/university/private partnerships to report on unevenly enforced police discipline, and how you could execute a similar project with the data and documents in your state. This panel was planned in conjunction with "Utilizing new police misconduct and employment databases," scheduled for Friday at 9:00 a.m. This panel will focus on changing laws, data partnerships and developing stories; the next panel will give specific how-tos using available data.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Story ideas | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Jodi Upton, Syracuse University — Jodi Upton is the Knight Chair at Syracuse University, teaching investigative/data journalism classes. She is a consultant for the Knight Commission and the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee. Her research includes college athletics’ spending and gender inequities in college sports, police misconduct in New York and mass killings. A transparency advocate, Upton and her students have filed more than 1,000 open records requests in recent years.
  • Sam Stecklow, Invisible Institute — Sam Stecklow is an investigative journalist and FOIA fellow with Invisible Institute, a nonprofit public accountability journalism organization based in Chicago. He works on investigations into police misconduct and abuse, and on II's public data tools the Civic Police Data Project and the National Police Index. His work has been recognized with a Sunshine Award from Utah SPJ and a Driehaus Award for investigative journalism. He is currently based in Orlando.
  • Sarah Taddeo, USA TODAY Network
  • David Robinson, Rochester Democrat and Chronicle
  • Katey Rusch, UC Berkeley — Katey Rusch is an investigative reporter at UC Berkeley’s Investigative Reporting Program, focused on police accountability and public records. A Pulitzer Prize finalist, she specializes in data-driven investigations.

Resources

  • Slides
  • Tipsheet

item.title: Google Trends for election coverage
item.description:
Learn how to use Google Trends to track search interest in real-time and support election coverage. We’ll show you how to watch for major spikes in searches, get national and regional-level data and use search insights to find inspiration for your next story. This session is sponsored by Google Trends. IRE retains control of content, including the topic and speaker selection, for all conference sessions.
Speakers: Alejandra Arevalo Zuniga, Google
item.content:encoded:
Learn how to use Google Trends to track search interest in real-time and support election coverage. We’ll show you how to watch for major spikes in searches, get national and regional-level data and use search insights to find inspiration for your next story. This session is sponsored by Google Trends. IRE retains control of content, including the topic and speaker selection, for all conference sessions.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Tools & Tech, Story ideas | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Alejandra Arevalo Zuniga, Google — Alejandra Arevalo is a data analyst at Google Trends. Previously, as a developer fellow at Hearst Newspapers, she built data, AI and visual storytelling products for local newsrooms across the U.S. She also worked at analytics company Chartmetric, exploring music industry trends through data. An active member of the data visualization community, Alejandra's work has been featured in Nightingale, the journal of the Data Visualization Society.
item.itunes:author: Alejandra Arevalo Zuniga, Google
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 10:00 PM ET

item.title: How to turn local data into far more useful tools for your newsroom — and your audiences
item.description:
Local data like election results, campaign finance, and school budgets can become core infrastructure for your newsroom, helping reporters add context and depth to their stories that no one else will have. And while they support your editorial department, those same data tools can also help your newsroom launch useful and engaging news products. Learn about two such projects underway in Lancaster County, Pa. and at The Texas Tribune and see how they are designing and building core data infrastructure to support stronger local government coverage.
Speakers: AmyJo Brown, The Public Ledger; Rob Reid, Texas Tribune; Jaden Edison, The Texas Tribune
item.content:encoded:
Local data like election results, campaign finance, and school budgets can become core infrastructure for your newsroom, helping reporters add context and depth to their stories that no one else will have. And while they support your editorial department, those same data tools can also help your newsroom launch useful and engaging news products. Learn about two such projects underway in Lancaster County, Pa. and at The Texas Tribune and see how they are designing and building core data infrastructure to support stronger local government coverage.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Public records | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • AmyJo Brown, The Public Ledger — AmyJo Brown is a veteran journalist with a background in local government and investigative reporting. As a consulting editor, she focuses on the organizational challenges specific to doing good journalism at the local level. She designed The Public Ledger — a strategy rooted in beat expertise and hard-won knowledge of newsroom economics — to transform public records into research infrastructure, helping newsrooms track money, map power & surface story leads faster.
  • Rob Reid, Texas Tribune — Rob Reid is the Texas Tribune's education data developer, building a K-12 education data warehouse and web tools while supporting reporters with data analysis. He joined in 2024 after a data journalism internship at the Jacksonville Tributary, Columbia University's Lede Program and data consulting for Northwestern's Medill School. In a prior career, he developed a case management database with Healthy Families America.
  • Jaden Edison, The Texas Tribune — Jaden Edison is an education reporter for the Texas Tribune, where he previously worked as a reporting fellow in summer 2022. Before returning to the Tribune full-time, he served as the justice reporter for The Connecticut Mirror, a nonprofit covering government, politics and public policy. Jaden has a master's degree from the Toni Stabile Center for Investigative Journalism at Columbia University and a bachelor's degree in journalism from Texas State University.
item.itunes:author: AmyJo Brown, The Public Ledger; Rob Reid, Texas Tribune; Jaden Edison, The Texas Tribune
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 8:45 PM ET

item.title: Data in the world of audio
item.description:
Are you a data-oriented reporter working at a public radio station? Interested in presenting findings from a data-driven investigation on a podcast or talk show? This session will debunk the common saying heard in many public radio newsrooms that "numbers and radio don't mix." This panel will provide practical tips on how to effectively present data-driven reporting in different audio mediums, ranging from long-form podcasts to short-form newscasts. It'll also be a open forum for discussing how reporters have found creative ways to put data journalism on air.
Speakers: Hannah Reale, GBH News; Clayton Guse, WNYC/Gothamist; Amy Qin, WBEZ
item.content:encoded:
Are you a data-oriented reporter working at a public radio station? Interested in presenting findings from a data-driven investigation on a podcast or talk show? This session will debunk the common saying heard in many public radio newsrooms that "numbers and radio don't mix." This panel will provide practical tips on how to effectively present data-driven reporting in different audio mediums, ranging from long-form podcasts to short-form newscasts. It'll also be a open forum for discussing how reporters have found creative ways to put data journalism on air.

Type: Panel | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: Data analysis, AI | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Hannah Reale, GBH News — Hannah Reale covers children and families in Massachusetts. She’s sampled journalism that touches just one New York neighborhood to every corner of the globe, reporting for NPR, The World, The Wire China, The West Side Rag and The Wesleyan Argus. As an editor, she works with journalists across the newsroom to improve web stories for structure, style and accuracy. She’s a determined newshound, shutterbug and eagle-eyed editor who ferrets out hard-to-wrangle datasets.
  • Clayton Guse, WNYC/Gothamist — Clayton Guse is the transportation and infrastructure editor at New York Public Radio, where he oversees coverage of pressing issues facing the country's largest city. He previously served as the transit reporter for the New York Daily News, leading coverage of mass transit in the city throughout the COVID-19 pandemic.
  • Amy Qin, WBEZ — Amy Qin is a data reporter for WBEZ, where she uses a combination of data analysis, computer programming and traditional reporting methods to tell data-driven stories for broadcast and print.
item.itunes:author: Hannah Reale, GBH News; Clayton Guse, WNYC/Gothamist; Amy Qin, WBEZ
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 8:45 PM ET

item.title: Accessing data on deaths in custody
item.description:
Thanks to recent FOIA lawsuits and the efforts of organizations like The Marshall Project, The Data Liberation Project, Behind Bars Data Project, and Mapping Police Violence, data on deaths in custody is more accessible than ever. This panel will guide reporters on how to find and use this data to investigate deaths in prisons, jails, and police custody. Attendees will learn about what data is available, what's missing from the data, and how the data can reveal problematic patterns in trends in the local law enforcement agencies.
Speakers: Ethan Corey, Behind Bars Data Project; A.J. Lagoe, KARE-TV; Ivy Scott, The Marshall Project
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1153.pdf
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1153-1.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to recent FOIA lawsuits and the efforts of organizations like The Marshall Project, The Data Liberation Project, Behind Bars Data Project, and Mapping Police Violence, data on deaths in custody is more accessible than ever. This panel will guide reporters on how to find and use this data to investigate deaths in prisons, jails, and police custody. Attendees will learn about what data is available, what's missing from the data, and how the data can reveal problematic patterns in trends in the local law enforcement agencies.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Ethan Corey, Behind Bars Data Project
  • A.J. Lagoe, KARE-TV — A.J. Lagoe is an investigative reporter for KARE-TV in Minneapolis. His reporting routinely leads to criminal convictions, legislative hearings, and new state and federal laws. A.J. is a multi-time recipient of journalism’s highest honors including Peabody, Polk, duPont Columbia and IRE awards.
  • Ivy Scott, The Marshall Project — Ivy Scott is an engagement reporter for The Marshall Project, responsible for producing community-driven, investigative and public service journalism about the criminal justice systems in St. Louis and Missouri. She was previously a beat reporter at The Boston Globe, covering climate solutions, state courts, police accountability and the Massachusetts Department of Correction. Her work has been honored as a Livingston finalist and Webby nominee.

Resources

  • Tipsheet
  • Slides

item.title: Supporting the next generation of journalists through a historic slide in U.S. press freedom
item.description:
As the next generation of journalists enters the field at a time when the Committee to Protect Journalists has warned that “press freedom in the United States is under siege,” we explore how student reporters are navigating that threatening climate on and off campus. We hear from student journalists whose universities have encroached on editorial independence or withdrawn institutional support. We also hear from a student journalist who was arrested and convicted for doing his job. As the industry reckons with this reality on a broad scale, we speak with a senior reporter at the U.S. Press Freedom Tracker and invite all in attendance to join us in a conversation as we ask: How can we best support our student colleagues under such conditions?
Speakers: Andrew Miller, Indiana Daily Student; Mia Hilkowitz, Indiana University; Lucas Griffith, University of Cincinnati; Olivia Mapes, The Purdue Exponent; Stephanie Sugars, U.S. Press Freedom Tracker; Elaine Monaghan, Indiana University
item.content:encoded:
As the next generation of journalists enters the field at a time when the Committee to Protect Journalists has warned that “press freedom in the United States is under siege,” we explore how student reporters are navigating that threatening climate on and off campus. We hear from student journalists whose universities have encroached on editorial independence or withdrawn institutional support. We also hear from a student journalist who was arrested and convicted for doing his job. As the industry reckons with this reality on a broad scale, we speak with a senior reporter at the U.S. Press Freedom Tracker and invite all in attendance to join us in a conversation as we ask: How can we best support our student colleagues under such conditions?

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Students, Story ideas, Career | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Andrew Miller, Indiana Daily Student — Andrew Miller has served as co-editor-in-chief for the Indiana Daily Student since August 2025. There, he’s previously held the positions of science and environmental reporter, elections reporter, news editor and managing editor. In summer 2024, he interned at The Oregonian in Portland, Oregon, reporting on business and the timber industry. In summer last year, he reported general assignment at The Herald-Times.
  • Mia Hilkowitz, Indiana University — Mia is a senior at Indiana University Bloomington studying journalism. She is co-editor-in-chief of the Indiana Daily Student and a board member and intern for the Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism.
  • Lucas Griffith, University of Cincinnati
  • Olivia Mapes, The Purdue Exponent — Olivia Mapes is the editor-in-chief of The Exponent, Purdue's independent newspaper. Mapes is a senior studying creative writing and literature and planning on becoming a full-time journalist. During her time at The Exponent she has broken the news on the restructuring of the honors college, which the university has still not publicly announced, and did a deep dive on the effects of an insurance change on university faculty.
  • Stephanie Sugars, U.S. Press Freedom Tracker — Stephanie is the managing reporter for the U.S. Press Freedom Tracker, where she oversees the newsroom and contributes to its coverage of press freedom violations across the United States. A graduate of New York University’s Global and Joint Program Studies program in journalism and international relations, her professional work focuses on human rights, politics and identity-targeted violence.
  • Elaine Monaghan, Indiana University — An award-winning teacher, Elaine Monaghan is a professor of practice in journalism at Indiana University-Bloomington. Elaine spent 20 years in the field, mostly as a foreign correspondent for Reuters in Moscow, Kyiv, Dublin, Kosovo and Washington. Elaine has blogged about the election of President Barack Obama for Microsoft UK, worked for Congressional Quarterly and CQ magazine, and was a Washington correspondent for The Times of London, where she also authored a column, Abroad in America.
item.itunes:author: Andrew Miller, Indiana Daily Student; Mia Hilkowitz, Indiana University; Lucas Griffith, University of Cincinnati; Olivia Mapes, The Purdue Exponent; Stephanie Sugars, U.S. Press Freedom Tracker; Elaine Monaghan, Indiana University
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: Next-gen crime data: Know your NIBRS!
item.description:
Forget murder, robbery and burglary. Do you know how many ATM thefts your local police logged last year? Or carjackings? Or stalking cases? Most police agencies now log a wealth of detail on 58 crimes via the National Incident-Based Reporting. Learn how to get started using this invaluable but complicated data.
Speakers: Paul Overberg, The Wall Street Journal; Jill Castellano, The Marshall Project; Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1129.pdf
Slides (2): https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1129-1.pdf
Slides (3): https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1129-2.pdf
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1129-3.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Forget murder, robbery and burglary. Do you know how many ATM thefts your local police logged last year? Or carjackings? Or stalking cases? Most police agencies now log a wealth of detail on 58 crimes via the National Incident-Based Reporting. Learn how to get started using this invaluable but complicated data.

Type: Panel | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: Tools & Tech, Research, Data analysis | Room: White River I

Speakers

  • Paul Overberg, The Wall Street Journal — Paul Overberg is a member of the data team at The Wall Street Journal, where he won an Emmy last year as part of a team that produced a video investigation of Tesla crashes. He worked on USA TODAY’s data team for many years. He also has taught at Northwestern's Medill School of Journalism and served as a senior fellow for the Center for Health Journalism at the University of Southern California.
  • Jill Castellano, The Marshall Project — Jill Castellano is a data journalist for The Marshall Project. She has worked for ABC Television, inewsource, The Desert Sun and The Salt Lake Tribune. Along with her colleagues, she was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for explanatory reporting in 2018 for her work with USA Today on the deaths of undocumented border crossers.
  • Maia Rosenfeld, KFF Health News

item.title: Introducing the new IRE Resource Center
item.description:
The new IRE Resource Center is here! Members can now access an enhanced, user-friendly search interface with more opportunities to easily surface thousands of tipsheets, contest entries and other training materials. In this session, the volunteers who built the app will demonstrate its new features, talk about how it was made and solicit live feedback from the audience.
Speakers: Ben Welsh, Reuters; Derek Willis, University of Maryland
Slides: https://palewi.re/docs/ire-archive-slide-deck/

item.title: Everyone can be (and should be) a housing reporter
item.description:
The housing crisis is touching communities of every size — not just coastal cities or low‑income renters. In this session, we’ll walk through practical data tools, sources, and reporting techniques to cover affordable housing challenges, evictions, and homelessness. Whether you’re new to the beat, jumping on a fast-moving daily story, or looking to deepen your expertise, you’ll learn accessible methods for finding, analyzing, and localizing housing data to produce clearer, more impactful reporting.
Speakers: Juan Pablo Garnham, Eviction Lab; Aaron Mondry, Outlier Media
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1007.pdf
item.content:encoded:
The housing crisis is touching communities of every size — not just coastal cities or low‑income renters. In this session, we’ll walk through practical data tools, sources, and reporting techniques to cover affordable housing challenges, evictions, and homelessness. Whether you’re new to the beat, jumping on a fast-moving daily story, or looking to deepen your expertise, you’ll learn accessible methods for finding, analyzing, and localizing housing data to produce clearer, more impactful reporting.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Juan Pablo Garnham, Eviction Lab — Juan Pablo Garnham is communication and policy engagement manager for the Eviction Lab. He is a reporter with experience covering housing, homelessness and urban issues across the United States and Latin America. His work has been featured by news outlets like The Texas Tribune, The Guardian, Bloomberg and Univision.
  • Aaron Mondry, Outlier Media — Aaron Mondry is a senior reporter with Outlier Media who covers stories about housing, real estate and development in Detroit.

Resources

  • Slides

item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 7:30 PM ET

item.title: AI concepts you should know
item.description:
AI, Machine Learning, Deep Learning ... what do these terms really mean, and what is the difference? Many journalists are still grappling with an explosion of buzzwords and concepts. This session aims to demystify the jargon and provide a clear, practical explanation of key AI terms and tools.
Speakers: Libby Seline, University of Chicago; Ryan Serpico, Hearst; Jon Keegan, Sherwood News
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1008.pdf
Website: https://aiglossary.news/
item.content:encoded:
AI, Machine Learning, Deep Learning ... what do these terms really mean, and what is the difference? Many journalists are still grappling with an explosion of buzzwords and concepts. This session aims to demystify the jargon and provide a clear, practical explanation of key AI terms and tools.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: AI | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Libby Seline, University of Chicago — Libby Seline is a University of Chicago graduate student studying computational analysis and public policy. Before grad school, she was a data reporter at the San Antonio Express-News via the Texas Development Hub, and then she worked at the Flatwater Free Press as their first election guide editor. She also has bylines in The New York Times, the Salt Lake Tribune and the Lincoln Journal Star.
  • Ryan Serpico, Hearst — Ryan Serpico is deputy director of newsroom AI and automation at Hearst Newspapers' DevHub, where he builds AI-powered tools that help journalists find stories and serve readers. His work includes Assembly/Meeting Monitor, winner of the 2024 Online Journalism Award, and Producer-P, used 1,000+ times monthly across Hearst properties. A University of Florida journalism graduate, he's focused on technology that amplifies reporters, not replaces them.
  • Jon Keegan, Sherwood News — Keegan is a data reporter covering AI and tech for Sherwood News. Previously, he was at The Markup and The Wall Street Journal.

Resources

  • Slides
  • Website

item.title: Using data to find sources
item.description:
Data can provide context, show trends, and debunk false claims. But journalists don't always recognize its power to elevate even the more traditional parts of reporting. Data analysis can help you find human sources (often with names and contact information in the spreadsheet itself). It can also help you pinpoint where to go for field reporting. We'll explore all these possibilities in this panel with a handful of examples you can take back to your newsroom.
Speakers: Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal; Aaron Mendelson, The Trace; Adriana Navarro, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting; Taylor Nichols, Houston Chronicle
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1022.pdf
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1022-1.pdf
item.content:encoded:
Data can provide context, show trends, and debunk false claims. But journalists don't always recognize its power to elevate even the more traditional parts of reporting. Data analysis can help you find human sources (often with names and contact information in the spreadsheet itself). It can also help you pinpoint where to go for field reporting. We'll explore all these possibilities in this panel with a handful of examples you can take back to your newsroom.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal — Brian Whitton is a computational journalist at The Wall Street Journal. He builds reporting tools and analyzes data for some of the Journal's most ambitious projects. If the numbers are big, he's probably involved somehow.
  • Aaron Mendelson, The Trace — Aaron Mendelson is a data journalist at The Trace, a nonprofit newsroom that covers gun violence. He works on The Trace’s Gun Violence Data Hub initiative. Previously, Mendelson was an investigative and data reporter at the Center for Public Integrity and NPR affiliate KPCC/LAist. His reporting has garnered honors including a Gerald Loeb Award, Online Journalism Awards and an IRE Award.
  • Adriana Navarro, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting — Adriana Navarro is a data journalist at the Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting, where she covers the intersection of gender, politics and public policy. Previously, Navarro worked on an award-winning investigative series examining privately sponsored travel and lobbying loopholes in Congress. Her work has appeared in The Baltimore Banner, Politico and The Washington Post.
  • Taylor Nichols, Houston Chronicle — Taylor is a data reporter covering education and local government for the Houston Chronicle. She holds a master’s degree in data journalism from the University of Maryland, where she worked for the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. Her reporting has taken her from covering housing in Maine and Washington, D.C. to writing about higher education nationwide. She's passionate about accountability journalism and building tools to make data easy to use and understand.

Resources

  • Slides
  • Tipsheet
item.itunes:author: Brian Whitton, The Wall Street Journal; Aaron Mendelson, The Trace; Adriana Navarro, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting; Taylor Nichols, Houston Chronicle

item.title: Investigating the AI data center boom
item.description:
This panel will discuss how to investigate the environmental and social impacts of new data centers being built in the US. Panelists will present strategies for revealing hidden costs of the data center boom, exploring how data centers strain resources and reshape rural communities, particularly in drought-stricken and farming regions.
Speakers: Michelle Ma, Bloomberg; Phoebe Liu, Forbes; Enrique Saenz, MirrorIndy
item.content:encoded:
This panel will discuss how to investigate the environmental and social impacts of new data centers being built in the US. Panelists will present strategies for revealing hidden costs of the data center boom, exploring how data centers strain resources and reshape rural communities, particularly in drought-stricken and farming regions.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas, Research | Room: White River I

Speakers

  • Michelle Ma, Bloomberg — Michelle Ma is a reporter for Bloomberg News in Los Angeles. She covers clean energy and climate technologies. She also writes about the climate impacts of the AI data center boom and the intersection of climate change, electric utilities and insurance in the western US. Previously, she was a climate reporter at Protocol. Before that, she was a news editor of live journalism and special coverage at The Wall Street Journal.
  • Phoebe Liu, Forbes
  • Enrique Saenz, MirrorIndy
item.itunes:author: Michelle Ma, Bloomberg; Phoebe Liu, Forbes; Enrique Saenz, MirrorIndy

item.title: Finding a needle in a haystack: How to manage document-heavy investigations
item.description:
Searching for relevant evidence for a story through a never-ending pile of documents, legal forms, contracts, emails, videos, or cell phone data can be taxing and time-consuming, even for the best investigative teams out there. The team at Everlaw will demonstrate their tools to help with automatic transcription of audio and video (think bodycam footage, depositions, interview recordings), making documents text-searchable through optical character recognition, file management tools (like search, labeling, AI summaries), analysis tools (like data viz and topic clustering), and story-building tools (drafting, citations, and AI writing assistance). They'll show how newsrooms like the San Francisco Chronicle, ProPublica and Fast Company uncover key information using their tool. You'll also learn about Everlaw’s free offering for journalists, which includes unlimited users, training and support at no extra cost.
Speakers: Chris Miles, Everlaw
item.content:encoded:
Searching for relevant evidence for a story through a never-ending pile of documents, legal forms, contracts, emails, videos, or cell phone data can be taxing and time-consuming, even for the best investigative teams out there. They'll show how newsrooms like the San Francisco Chronicle, ProPublica and Fast Company uncover key information using their tool. You'll also learn about Everlaw’s free offering for journalists, which includes unlimited users, training and support at no extra cost.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Tools & Tech | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Chris Miles, Everlaw — Chris works at the intersection of technology and journalism, and he is an expert at helping bridge the gap between cutting-edge tech and the reporters who rely on it. At Meta, CrowdTangle and Everlaw, he has built and scaled products used by newsrooms worldwide, led complex partnerships and advised journalists and researchers navigating the rapidly evolving information space.
item.itunes:author: Chris Miles, Everlaw
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 4:30 PM ET

item.title: The SLAPP Back Initiative: Building a first-of-its-kind database
item.description:
The SLAPP Back Initiative: Building a first-of-its-kind databaseHave you considered building a database of your own but are not sure where to start? Join lead researcher Susanna Granieri of The SLAPP Back Initiative, a first-of-its-kind database tracking strategic lawsuits against public participation, meritless and frivolous actions used to chill speech and deter scrutiny. In this demo session, Susanna will outline the team's steps from inception to launch: What research tools were used? How was the methodology developed? How did they interrogate the data and bulletproof their findings? What questions have they answered, and what questions remain? She'll also show you how, as the database grows, it can help empower your journalism, identifying common offenders and frequent targets in your state.
Speakers: Susanna Granieri, First Amendment Watch
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1334.pdf

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Research | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Susanna Granieri, First Amendment Watch — Susanna Granieri is a reporter and researcher for First Amendment Watch. She previously reported on politics and policy for the Albany-based Legislative Gazette and investigated the use of faulty forensic science in death penalty convictions with the Mississippi Center for Investigative Reporting. She is a graduate of SUNY New Paltz and the Columbia Journalism School.

item.title: Reporting on maternal and infant health in turbulent times
item.description:
The U.S. has the highest maternal mortality rate of any high-income nation, with stark differences within the U.S. along racial and geographic lines. Early evidence suggests that system shocks like Covid and state-level abortion bans may have made pregnancy and childbirth even more dangerous. Meanwhile, the Trump administration has slashed public health initiatives, altered childhood vaccine schedules and gutted many federal data collection projects, including those focusing on maternal and infant health outcomes. This panel will explore the ways we can use available data to report on maternal and infant health, and discuss ways we can continue this work at the state, regional and national level despite the loss of some federal data sources.
Speakers: Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica; Sophie Chou, Independent journalist; Holly Hacker, KFF Health News
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1158-1.pdf
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1158.pdf
item.content:encoded:
The U.S. has the highest maternal mortality rate of any high-income nation, with stark differences within the U.S. along racial and geographic lines. Early evidence suggests that system shocks like Covid and state-level abortion bans may have made pregnancy and childbirth even more dangerous. Meanwhile, the Trump administration has slashed public health initiatives, altered childhood vaccine schedules and gutted many federal data collection projects, including those focusing on maternal and infant health outcomes. This panel will explore the ways we can use available data to report on maternal and infant health, and discuss ways we can continue this work at the state, regional and national level despite the loss of some federal data sources.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Story ideas | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica — Andrea Suozzo is a news apps developer at ProPublica, where she runs Nonprofit Explorer and does data reporting on nonprofits, maternal health and other topics.
  • Sophie Chou, Independent journalist — Sophie Chou is an award-winning data journalist and researcher with over a decade of experience. Her reporting on maternal deaths and complications following the abortion ban in Texas contributed to ProPublica’s Pulitzer Prize for Public Service. Trained in computer science, she has a graduate degree from the MIT Media Lab.
  • Holly Hacker, KFF Health News — Holly is data editor at KFF Health News, a national nonprofit newsroom dedicated to health policy and health care coverage. Previously, she was an investigative reporter at The Dallas Morning News. She lives in Maryland.

Resources

  • Slides
  • Tipsheet
item.itunes:author: Andrea Suozzo, ProPublica; Sophie Chou, Independent journalist; Holly Hacker, KFF Health News

item.title: Pitching, reporting and planning the investigation
item.description:
We'll talk about how to find, develop and report quick-turn and enterprise projects. Topics include vetting ideas, time management and communicating with your editor and others in the newsroom. We'll also look at a litmus test for whether a story is worth further investigation.
Speakers: Francisco Vara-Orta, Investigative Reporters & Editors; Diana Fuentes, Investigative Reporters & Editors

item.title: From delving into data to door knocking: Finding people behind the numbers and getting them to speak
item.description:
You've finished your FOIAs. You've done the data analysis. You've talked with experts. Now you're ready to put your story together. There's just one problem. You haven't yet spoken to the people who are impacted by the findings found in your research. In this panel, journalists from across the media landscape talk about ways they've found people who are impacted by the topics they were covering and/or data they were researching. Plus, once you've found the people, how do you convince them to go on the record? The panelists are here to answer your questions and talk about how they centered their stories around the people they found.
Speakers: Jared Kofsky, ABC News; Stephanie Lamm, Atlanta Journal-Constitution; Caresse Jackman, InvestigateTV; Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media; Mark Nichols, ABC News
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1009.pdf
item.content:encoded:
You've finished your FOIAs. You've done the data analysis. You've talked with experts. Now you're ready to put your story together. There's just one problem. You haven't yet spoken to the people who are impacted by the findings found in your research. In this panel, journalists from across the media landscape talk about ways they've found people who are impacted by the topics they were covering and/or data they were researching. Plus, once you've found the people, how do you convince them to go on the record? The panelists are here to answer your questions and talk about how they centered their stories around the people they found.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Story ideas | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Jared Kofsky, ABC News — Jared Kofsky produces investigative and breaking news stories for ABC News platforms, including Good Morning America, World News Tonight with David Muir, ABC News Live, This Week with George Stephanopoulos, Nightline and ABCNews.com, working from both the field and the network’s headquarters in New York City. He is also an on-air contributor for ABC News Radio. Jared previously covered news in South Carolina and New Jersey.
  • Stephanie Lamm, Atlanta Journal-Constitution — Stephanie Lamm is a data reporter at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution who uses public records, data analysis and shoe-leather reporting to hold institutions accountable and uncover information that would otherwise remain hidden. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and spent five years working in Texas before landing in Atlanta.
  • Caresse Jackman, InvestigateTV — Caresse is an award-winning national consumer investigative reporter based in Washington D.C. with Gray Media’s InvestigateTV. Prior to InvestigateTV, she was a consumer investigative reporter at WSMV-TV in Nashville, TN. She has prior reporting experience at WWL-TV in New Orleans, WJRT in Flint, Michigan, and at WCBI in Columbus, Mississippi. She started her career behind the scenes as a producer at WJTV in Jackson, Mississippi.
  • Elisabeth Gawthrop, American Public Media — Elisabeth is a data journalist with American Public Media and a visiting journalist-in-residence at Indiana University.
  • Mark Nichols, ABC News — Mark Nichols is a senior manager of data journalism for ABC News, developing data-driven investigative stories and projects for ABC's World News Tonight, Nightline, Good Morning America, Live, and digital content for abcnews.com. He also works with the data team that serves ABC's Owned Television Station affiliates in eight major U.S. cities.

Resources

  • Slides

item.title: Accessing records to report on immigration enforcement actions
item.description:
Data sets and public records offer critical context for coverage of immigration enforcement actions taking place in communities across the country, but it’s often difficult to know where to look for this information or how to respond when access is denied. This session will provide practical tips for journalists on how to obtain data and records for reporting on immigration issues, and the resources available to push for greater transparency when necessary.
Speakers: Gunita Singh, Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP); Will Craft, The Guardian; Eileen Grench, Documented; Jim Daley, South Side Weekly; Pratheek Rebala, ProPublica
item.content:encoded:
Data sets and public records offer critical context for coverage of immigration enforcement actions taking place in communities across the country, but it’s often difficult to know where to look for this information or how to respond when access is denied. This session will provide practical tips for journalists on how to obtain data and records for reporting on immigration issues, and the resources available to push for greater transparency when necessary.

Type: Panel | Level: Intermediate | Tracks: Data analysis, Story ideas | Room: White River I

  • Will Craft, The Guardian — Will Craft is a data editor at the Guardian.

  • Jim Daley, South Side Weekly

item.title: Using data to investigate the global surveillance industry
item.description:
Around the world, shadowy firms are siphoning location data from apps, telecom networks, and rogue brokers to track people with chilling precision. Whether it’s journalists, Silicon Valley executives, or visitors to Jeffrey Epstein’s island, no one is off-limits. This panel brings together journalists who have exposed how invisible surveillance infrastructure is being used to follow people worldwide—and how little stands in the way. The journalists will explain how they uncovered these stories, how they obtained covert datasets through techniques ranging from identifying misconfigured cloud storage buckets to scouring undocumented APIs. They’ll break down how journalists can use data to expose the inner workings of the shadowy tracking systems and offer practical methods for investigating the rapidly expanding global surveillance industry.
Speakers: Aaron Sankin, The Marshall Project; Andrew Couts, WIRED
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1069.pdf

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Data analysis, Data viz, Beat reporting | Room: White River E

Speakers

  • Gabriel Geiger, Lighthouse Reports
  • Aaron Sankin, The Marshall Project — Aaron Sankin is the data editor at The Marshall Project, a nonprofit news organization covering the criminal justice system in America. He was previously an investigative reporter at The Markup and the Center for Investigative Reporting.
  • Andrew Couts, WIRED — Andrew Couts is Senior Editor, Security & Investigations at WIRED, overseeing cybersecurity, privacy, policy, national security, and surveillance coverage. He also oversees investigations across WIRED's newsroom.

Resources

item.title: How to get data out of complex documents using AI
item.description:
Learn how newsrooms get important information out of large volumes of documents (pdfs, audio transcripts) and vet the results to power reporting.
Speakers: Disha Raychaudhuri, Reuters; Duy Nguyen, The New York Times; Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times
item.content:encoded:
Learn how newsrooms get important information out of large volumes of documents (pdfs, audio transcripts) and vet the results to power reporting.

Type: Demo | Level: Beginner | Tracks: AI, Tools & Tech | Room: White River D

Speakers

  • Disha Raychaudhuri, Reuters — Disha Raychaudhuri is a data journalist at Reuters covering global inequality, race and social justice. She is also an active member of the Reuters Peer Network, offering peer‑to‑peer mental health support to colleagues. Before joining Reuters in 2020, she was a data reporter at The Star‑Ledger, covering demographics, inequality and policing. She moved to the U.S. in 2016 after reporting stints in India and Bangladesh focused on education and oral‑history projects.
  • Duy Nguyen, The New York Times — Duy Nguyen is a data scientist and engineer for The New York Times A.I. Initiatives team, where he leads original research and tooling to unlock new reporting and production capabilities, while maintaining responsible A.I. use. Before that, he helped local media solve business and audience problems with A.I. at Columbia Journalism School. He was previously a data journalist at The Times and NPR and briefly contributed to DocumentCloud.
  • Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times
item.itunes:author: Disha Raychaudhuri, Reuters; Duy Nguyen, The New York Times; Teresa Mondría Terol, The New York Times
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 2:00 PM ET

item.title: Following the money in college sports’ new era
item.description:
College sports have changed faster in the past five years than in the previous five decades. Join us as we break down the changes in college athletics and look at what’s coming, all of which makes accurate data critical, especially financial data. We’ll walk through the college finance data, explain new categories available and how to use them (and how not to). We’ll also discuss how journalists can secure information about athlete name, image, and likeness deals obtained via Freedom of Information requests. Panelists will also explore current and emerging financial trends in the industry, highlighting issues that journalists across the country should be monitoring, including the $1B in severance paid to get coaches NOT to coach. We'll cover rising student fees, challenges to equity, and new athlete revenue-sharing payments. The panelists will also discuss other stories of interest in the future of college sports, including the prospect of Congressional intervention and the possibility that college athletes become employees. This panel is especially suitable for college students or anyone looking for college sports data and trying to navigate the new rules.
Speakers: Jodi Upton, Syracuse University; Max Rivera, Bloomberg; Peyton Barish, Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database; Michael H. LeRoy, University of Illinois
item.content:encoded:
College sports have changed faster in the past five years than in the previous five decades. Join us as we break down the changes in college athletics and look at what’s coming, all of which makes accurate data critical, especially financial data. We’ll walk through the college finance data, explain new categories available and how to use them (and how not to). We’ll also discuss how journalists can secure information about athlete name, image, and likeness deals obtained via Freedom of Information requests. Panelists will also explore current and emerging financial trends in the industry, highlighting issues that journalists across the country should be monitoring, including the $1B in severance paid to get coaches NOT to coach. We'll cover rising student fees, challenges to equity, and new athlete revenue-sharing payments. The panelists will also discuss other stories of interest in the future of college sports, including the prospect of Congressional intervention and the possibility that college athletes become employees. This panel is especially suitable for college students or anyone looking for college sports data and trying to navigate the new rules.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Beat reporting, Sports, Story ideas | Room: White River B

Speakers

  • Jodi Upton, Syracuse University — Jodi Upton is the Knight Chair at Syracuse University, teaching investigative/data journalism classes. She is a consultant for the Knight Commission and the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee. Her research includes college athletics’ spending and gender inequities in college sports, police misconduct in New York and mass killings. A transparency advocate, Upton and her students have filed more than 1,000 open records requests in recent years.
  • Max Rivera, Bloomberg — Max Rivera is a data journalist. He’s a reporter at Bloomberg News, where he uses code to dig through large datasets and uncover stories across a wide range of beats. His reporting has ranged from immigration and automotive safety to sports.
  • Peyton Barish, Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database — Peyton Barish is the assistant project manager for the Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database and a former Division I athlete at NC State University. He has a background in college athletics policy with a particular interest in labor policy and innovative sport solutions.
  • Michael H. LeRoy, University of Illinois
item.itunes:author: Jodi Upton, Syracuse University; Max Rivera, Bloomberg; Peyton Barish, Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database; Michael H. LeRoy, University of Illinois
item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 2:00 PM ET

item.title: Data journalism for new reporters
item.description:
"If you build it - they will come": Many local reporters might not have the time and/or knowledge to incorporate data skills to their work. So how do you get into thinking and then doing data journalism? This session will include examples and ideas on where to start and how to work to raise awareness about data and how to use it. Over the next few days, you'll see there is a seemingly endless number of things you can learn in the data journalism world. It can be daunting! But everyone at this conference started at the same place as you. We'll help you get started! This session will cover the basics of data journalism, starting with what it is, tips for building up your skills and advice for how you can — realistically — integrate these skills into your daily reporting.
Speakers: Helena Bengtsson, Gota Media / Bonnier News Local; Jennifer LaFleur, UC Berkeley School of Journalism; Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News

"If you build it - they will come": Many local reporters might not have the time and/or knowledge to incorporate data skills to their work. So how do you get into thinking and then doing data journalism? This session will include examples and ideas on where to start and how to work to raise awareness about data and how to use it. Over the next few days, you'll see there is a seemingly endless number of things you can learn in the data journalism world. It can be daunting! But everyone at this conference started at the same place as you. We'll help you get started! This session will cover the basics of data journalism, starting with what it is, tips for building up your skills and advice for how you can — realistically — integrate these skills into your daily reporting.

Type: Panel | Level: Beginner | Tracks: Local | Room: White River G

Speakers

  • Helena Bengtsson, Gota Media / Bonnier News Local
  • Jennifer LaFleur, UC Berkeley School of Journalism
  • Cheryl Phillips, Big Local News — Cheryl Phillips is a lecturer at Stanford and founder of Big Local News, which supports newsrooms by sharing public data for accountability reporting. Last year, Big Local News contributed to a Pulitzer Prize for Local Reporting with The Baltimore Banner, The New York Times and nine other newsrooms. She spent 12 years at The Seattle Times, where she was a part of two Pulitzers for breaking news and two Pulitzer finalists. She is a former IRE board president.

item.itunes:subtitle: Thursday, March 5, 2:00 PM ET

item.title: Catching the quiet changes: Automating website monitoring for investigative journalism with Visualping
item.description:
In this session, you'll learn how to use Visualping, an AI website change detection tool, to automatically monitor and get instant alerts for important changes to any websites you need to track for your beat, whether it's local court dockets, government agency websites, a politician's social media pages, or something else. We'll walk through setting up a Visualping alert, using different features and settings, and tips for using Visualping's AI criteria and summary features. Attendees are welcome to sign up for a free Visualping account at visualping.io to follow along.
Speakers: Kayla Zhu, Visualping
Slides: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1348.pdf
Tipsheet: https://resources.ire.org/tipsheets/20260305-1348-1.pdf
